Radiography is currently the preferred modality over ultrasonography for diagnosing a fracture in an adult. Because ligament and meniscal testing is negative, MRI is not indicated. Arthrocentesis with culture and Gram stain would be indicated if the history and physical examination suggest an infection of the joint space.

QuestionStem: A 31-year-old gravida 3 para 2 at 8 weeks' gestation presents to the urgent care clinic because of a 2-day history of headache and fever. This morning she removed an engorged tick from her leg. On examination she is ill appearing but alert and interactive. An HEENT examination is normal and there is no pain with neck flexion. A cardiopulmonary examination is normal. There is no rash. You suspect Rocky Mountain spotted fever. In addition to supportive care, which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment? A) No additional treatment B) Amoxicillin C) Chloramphenicol D) Doxycycline E) Erythromycin
Explanation: Doxycycline is the recommended treatment for all individuals with presumed Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF). RMSF can be fatal, so testing for the condition is not recommended because it would delay treatment. Short courses (5â€“10 days) of doxycycline have been shown to be safe in children and pregnant persons. Inpatient treatment with rapid desensitization is recommended for individuals with a true allergy to doxycycline. Chloramphenicol is another treatment option for RMSF, but it is associated with a higher risk for death than doxycycline. There is no role for amoxicillin or erythromycin in the treatment of RMSF.

QuestionStem: A 53-year-old female with no significant past medical history presents to the emergency department with fatigue, pruritus, and right upper quadrant discomfort. A physical examination reveals mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory Findings Alkaline phosphatase 450 U/L (N 44â€“147) AST 70 U/L (N 10â€“40) Total bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL (N 0.1â€“1.2) Antimitochondrial antibodies positive Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Autoimmune hepatitis B) Drug-induced liver injury C) Metabolic dysfunctionâ€“associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) (nonalcoholic fatty liver disease) D) Primary biliary cholangitis E) Primary sclerosing cholangitis
Explanation: Primary biliary cholangitis is characterized by chronic cholestasis, positive antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA), and elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP). The American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases (AASLD) guidelines state that the diagnosis of primary biliary cholangitis can be established with the presence of at least 2 of the following: biochemical evidence of cholestasis (elevated ALP), presence of AMA, and histopathologic evidence of nonsuppurative destructive cholangitis. Elevated AMA is particularly helpful as it is found in >95% of cases of primary biliary cholangitis. Autoimmune hepatitis usually presents with elevated aminotransferases and positive autoantibodies such as antinuclear antibodies or antiâ€“smooth muscle antibodies, rather than AMA. It is not primarily a cholestatic disease and elevated ALP is typically not the predominant finding. Depending on the instigating agent, drug-induced liver injury can present with a variety of liver enzyme abnormalities; however, the presence of positive AMA and the chronicity of symptoms make it less likely in this patient scenario. Metabolic dysfunctionâ€“associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) (formerly known as nonalcoholic fatty liver disease) typically presents with elevated aminotransferases and is associated with metabolic syndrome. It is not characterized by cholestasis or positive AMA. Primary sclerosing cholangitis typically presents with cholestatic liver enzyme abnormalities and is often associated with inflammatory bowel disease. It is diagnosed based on characteristic imaging findings of bile duct strictures using MRI cholangiography or endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Positive AMA is rarely found in primary sclerosing cholangitis.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 28-year-old female presents to your office because she is concerned about changes in her mood. For the last few months she has been intermittently irritable and unable to sit still. She has had some weight loss, is not sleeping well, and feels fatigued. She has been working extra hours to get ahead of her office deadlines. She has maxed out her credit card and repeatedly cheated on her husband, who filed for divorce 2 weeks ago. She now feels depressed and anxious, and has considered suicide but does not have suicidal ideations today. She says that she has had similar episodes in the past. Her father left when she was an infant and her mother is incarcerated, and the patient is unsure of their medical history. Today the patient's vital signs, a CBC, a comprehensive metabolic panel, and TSH and vitamin B levels are unremarkable. A urine pregnancy test and drug screen are negative. 12 She is alert and oriented during the office visit. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate therapy at this time? A) Bupropion B) Paroxetine (Paxil) C) Quetiapine (Seroquel) D) Sertraline (Zoloft) E) Electroconvulsive therapy
Explanation: This patient has features of bipolar disorder, a mental health condition affecting >1% of the world's population. The DSM-5 criteria can be used to suggest a prognosis and determine episode severity. Patients with bipolar disorder have a high suicide rate, so proper diagnosis and prompt treatment are important. The risk of relapse increases in patients who are untreated. Treatment improves prognosis and medications are the mainstay of therapy. FDA-approved medications for the treatment of bipolar disorder include lithium, anticonvulsants, and atypical antipsychotics such as quetiapine. Although this patient has symptoms of depression, monotherapy with an antidepressant is contraindicated in patients with mixed features, manic episodes, or bipolar I disorder. Augmenting therapies such as omega-3 fatty acids, light therapy, ketamine, and probiotics may be helpful but current evidence is insufficient. Cognitive behavioral therapy, exercise, and nutrition have been shown to be beneficial as well. Electroconvulsive therapy can be an effective treatment for psychotic depression, mania, refractory illness, catatonia, acute psychosis, and suicidal behavior. However, pharmacotherapy is the first-line treatment for bipolar disorder.

QuestionStem: A 23-year-old female presents for IUD removal because she wants to conceive. She has never been pregnant, is in good health, and takes no medications. Her blood pressure is normal and her BMI is 33 kg/m2. Which one of the following should you recommend regarding aspirin prophylaxis for preeclampsia? A) No aspirin as she does not meet criteria B) Aspirin for 1â€“3 months prior to conception C) Aspirin at 12 weeks' gestation D) Aspirin if her blood pressure during pregnancy is >135/80 mm Hg
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommend aspirin prophylaxis for pregnant patients who are at increased risk for preeclampsia. Indications include 1 or more high-risk factors or 2 or more moderate-risk factors. High-risk factors include a history of preeclampsia, multifetal gestation, kidney disease, autoimmune disease, type 1 or type 2 diabetes, and chronic hypertension. Moderate-risk factors include first pregnancy, maternal age â‰¥35 years, BMI >30 kg/m2, family history of preeclampsia, sociodemographic characteristics, and personal history factors. This patient is nulliparous and has a pre-pregnancy BMI >30 kg/m2, giving her 2 moderate- risk factors. The recommended aspirin prophylaxis for preeclampsia is 81 mg per day and should be started at 12 weeks' gestation. Low-dose aspirin has been shown to decrease the frequency of preeclampsia as well as other adverse pregnancy outcomes such as intrauterine growth restriction and preterm birth. It also has favorable fetal and maternal safety profiles.

QuestionStem: A 74-year-old female presents to your office for follow-up of cardioembolic stroke 1 day after being discharged from the hospital. She presented to the hospital with acute left-sided hand and face weakness. An evaluation in the hospital revealed new atrial fibrillation, mild mitral regurgitation, and a small right-sided cerebral infarction. Cardioembolic stroke was diagnosed. She was discharged 48 hours later after her left-sided weakness had almost completely resolved. Which one of the following therapeutic options is preferred for this patient? A) Apixaban (Eliquis) B) Aspirin C) Clopidogrel (Plavix) D) Warfarin E) Aspirin plus clopidogrel
Reference: Kleindorfer DO, Towfighi A, Chaturvedi S, et al. 2021 guideline for the prevention of stroke in patients with stroke and transient ischemic attack: a guideline from the American Heart Association/American Stroke Association. Stroke. 2021;52(7):e364-e467.
Explanation: This patient presents with a recent history of cardioembolic stroke. In patients who have a TIA or small cardioembolic stroke and nonvalvular atrial fibrillation, guidelines recommend starting anticoagulation without antiplatelet agents 2â€“14 days after the event. Nonvalvular atrial fibrillation is defined by the lack of moderate to severe mitral stenosis or a mechanical heart valve; direct-acting oral anticoagulants are preferred to warfarin in these patients. The 2024 Optimal Timing of Anticoagulation After Acute Ischaemic Stroke (OPTIMAS) trial confirmed the safety of starting anticoagulation within 4 days of a stroke. In patients with larger strokes that may be at high risk for hemorrhagic conversion, initiating anticoagulation may be delayed to 14 days.

QuestionStem: A 75-year-old female with an ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) diagnosed in the emergency department has been admitted to your service. She underwent a percutaneous coronary intervention with a drug-eluting stent to the circumflex artery and had been recovering well until she developed acute shortness of breath on the fourth day of hospitalization. Today, her vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 91/55 mm Hg, a heart rate of 110 beats/min, and an oxygen saturation of 89% on room air. On physical examination, the patient appears moderately distressed and has no jugular venous pulse. Auscultation reveals a regular rate, normal S and S , and a systolic murmur over the mitral post. Crackles 1 2 are heard in all lung fields. There is no lower extremity edema. Which one of the following is the likely etiology of this condition? A) Cardiac tamponade B) Papillary muscle rupture C) Pericarditis D) Pulmonary embolism E) Supraventricular tachyarrhythmia
Explanation: Advances in angiography, pharmacology, and systems of care have improved morbidity and mortality for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) over the last several decades. This has reduced the incidence of mechanical complications of AMI; however, the hospital mortality for these complications, such as acute papillary muscle rupture, remains high. Acute papillary muscle rupture occurs 3â€“5 days after a transmural infarction involving the right coronary artery or circumflex artery, depending on coronary anatomy. This leads to acute mitral regurgitation, resulting in flash pulmonary edema and shock, as seen in this patient. Cardiac tamponade can occur after an AMI if the ventricular free wall ruptures, which is rare. Clinical clues include chest pain, shortness of breath, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins. Chest pain is also a core feature of pericarditis, making it an unlikely diagnosis in this patient. Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a common complication seen in immobile hospitalized patients and can be reduced with appropriate mechanical prophylaxis and chemoprophylaxis. While astute clinicians may rule out PE in this patient, her admission has likely included anticoagulation, and lack of edema makes a venous thromboembolism etiology for PE less likely. Arrhythmias can also occur after an AMI. Supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation may present similarly to this patient, but a ventricular rate of 110 beats/min is unlikely to result in hemodynamic compromise as described in this case.

QuestionStem: A 14-year-old male sees you to establish care. He has a past medical history of asthma and uses an albuterol inhaler (Proventil, Ventolin) as needed. He normally has symptoms about 3â€“4 days per week. Recent pulmonary function tests showed mildly reduced lung function. Based on the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) guidelines, which one of the following would be the preferred treatment strategy for this patient? A) Continuing as-needed albuterol B) Switching to as-needed formoterol C) Switching to an as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) plus formoterol D) Continuing as-needed albuterol, and adding a low-dose ICS for maintenance E) Continuing as-needed albuterol, and adding a low-dose ICS plus salmeterol for maintenance
Reference: Global Initiative for Asthma. Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. Updated 2022.
Explanation: Based on the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) guideline, patients who have symptoms <3â€“5 days per week with normal or mildly reduced lung function are classified as having mild asthma. For patients 12 years and older with mild asthma, the guidelines recommend treatment with as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus formoterol (ICS/formoterol). As-needed low-dose ICS/formoterol reduces the risk of severe asthma exacerbations by 60%â€“64% compared to as-needed short-acting Î²-agonists (SABAs) such as albuterol. ICS/formoterol also reduces the risk of severe exacerbations compared to other regimens that include a SABA reliever. Formoterol should not be used without an ICS.

QuestionStem: A 37-year-old male who recently returned from a trip to Vietnam presents for evaluation of an intensely pruritic rash on his feet. Physical examination findings are normal other than an erythematous, serpiginous rash on his feet and lower legs. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Cutaneous larva migrans B) Cutaneous leishmaniasis C) Herpesvirus B D) Neisseria meningitidis E) Scabies
Explanation: The most likely diagnosis is cutaneous larva migrans. The typical rash that occurs with this infection is an intensely pruritic, erythematous, serpiginous rash. It is typically seen on the feet and gluteal areas. There are no diagnostic tests for this infection and the diagnosis is made clinically. Infections are generally self-limited but can be treated with albendazole. Cutaneous larva migrans infections are mainly found in Asia and Africa where macaque monkeys are the reservoir. It has an incubation period of 1â€“5 days but can take up to 1 month. Cutaneous leishmaniasis generally presents with small papules that develop into nonhealing open sores with a raised border and central ulceration. The lesions are generally painless. Herpesvirus B presents with a vesicular rash near the site of inoculation and influenza-like symptoms. The clinical manifestations of Neisseria meningitidis range from asymptomatic dermatologic findings such as petechiae to severe systemic disease. Scabies presents with a very pruritic rash that is red with small bumps, typically appears along skinfolds, and often forms a line.

QuestionStem: A 10-year-old male is brought to your office by his mother for follow-up of treatment for a Cryptosporidium infection that he likely contracted during a camping trip after swimming in a freshwater lake. He has completed treatment and is asymptomatic. His mother asks when he is allowed to resume swimming. Because of concerns for ongoing asymptomatic shedding, you should recommend that the patient may return to swimming A) 3 days after treatment is completed B) 1 week after treatment is completed C) 3 days after symptoms resolve D) 1 week after symptoms resolve E) 2 weeks after symptoms resolve
Explanation: Infection with Cryptosporidium is caused by ingesting oocysts, which can survive for up to 10 days in treated water. Diarrhea is the predominant symptom, and a stool sample is used for diagnosis. Treatment for infection with Cryptosporidium is supportive with fluid and electrolyte replacement. Nitazoxanide can be used in cases of severe or prolonged disease. Because shedding of oocysts continues even after symptoms resolve, it is recommended that patients refrain from swimming for 2 weeks after resolution of symptoms.

QuestionStem: A previously healthy 27-year-old female at 36 weeks' gestation presents to the emergency department (ED) in a rural town. She reports a 1-week history of headaches, nausea, fatigue, and right upper quadrant pain. She has been taking acetaminophen for pain with minimal improvement in her symptoms, which have worsened today. On examination she has a temperature of 36.8 Â°C (98.2 Â°F), a blood pressure of 150/95 mm Hg, a respiratory rate of 18/min, and a pulse rate of 104 beats/min. There are no signs of distress on neonatal tracing. Laboratory Findings WBCs 12,400/mm3 (N 4000â€“11,000) Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (N 12.0â€“15.0) Platelets 43,000/mm3 (N 150,000â€“400,000) AST 112 U/L (N 10â€“59) ALT 95 U/L (N 10â€“28) Serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (N 0.6â€“1.1) Urine protein to creatinine ratio 422 mg/g (N <300) Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Administering oral prednisone B) Administering N-acetylcysteine C) Consultation with a gastroenterologist D) Arranging for urgent delivery
Explanation: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count (HELLP) syndrome should be suspected in pregnant women presenting with malaise, headache, hypertension, and proteinuria. Women with HELLP syndrome have 10%â€“15% maternal morbidity and mortality rates compared to those without. The elevated risks are due to the potential of severe complications such as cerebral hemorrhage, cardiopulmonary arrest, disseminated intravascular coagulation, acute respiratory distress syndrome, renal failure, sepsis, and hepatic hemorrhage. Early diagnosis and management are critical. Quick and targeted evaluation with urgent delivery in patients over 34 weeks' gestation is key to avoiding maternal deaths. Corticosteroids can be used to treat low platelet count, but will not address the symptoms of liver failure. Acute liver failure due to HELLP is secondary to pregnancy. Although this patient has been taking acetaminophen, the examination and laboratory results are consistent with HELLP syndrome; therefore, treatment for acetaminophen overdose with N-acetylcysteine is not warranted.

QuestionStem: A 64-year-old female with a history of hypertension and no prior evidence of kidney disease presents for follow-up of a recent basic metabolic panel that showed an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of 49 mL/min/1.73 m2. Which one of the following would confirm a diagnosis of chronic kidney disease? A) A urine albumin to creatinine ratio >30 mg/g at this time B) A urine albumin to creatinine ratio >300 mg/g at this time C) An additional eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m2 in 1 month D) An additional eGFR <90 mL/min/1.73 m2 in 2 months E) An additional eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m2 in 3 months
Reference: Darby JB, Jackson JM: Kawasaki disease and multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children: An overview and comparison. Am Fam Physician 2021;104(3):244-252.
Explanation: Before a diagnosis of chronic kidney disease can be made, evidence of renal impairment or damage must be present for 3 months in order to differentiate the renal abnormalities from an acute kidney injury (AKI). This is most often an estimated glomerular filtration rate <60 mL/min/1.73 m2; however, the Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) guidelines do allow for other evidence of persistent renal damage or decreased function, such as albuminuria or proteinuria, that persists for at least 3 months. Previous documentation of kidney damage or impairment can also be sought. Renal imaging abnormalities such as decreased kidney volume, cortical thickening, or multiple cysts are also evidence of chronicity.

QuestionStem: A 4-year-old male presents with a 6-day history of fever, rash, and an enlarged lymph node. On examination, you note erythema of the lips, bilateral conjunctivitis, and a diffuse maculopapular rash on the trunk and extremities. The right anterior cervical lymph node is nontender and 2 cm in size. Which one of the following is the most serious complication of this patient's condition? A) Cardiac B) Hematologic C) Hepatic D) Pulmonary E) Renal
Explanation: This patient presents with Kawasaki disease, which commonly occurs in children <5 years of age. Classic Kawasaki disease presents with 4 of the following features: oral mucous membrane changes, bilateral conjunctivitis, diffuse rash, hand and foot edema, and unilateral cervical adenopathy â‰¥1.5 cm. Inflammation occurs and can affect multiple organ systems. The most serious complication is cardiac. If untreated, 15%â€“25% of patients will develop cardiovascular abnormalities, including coronary artery dilation, aortic root dilation, cardiac aneurysms, tachycardia, hypotension, and shock. Echocardiography must be performed as soon as possible. Less serious complications of Kawasaki disease include anemia, elevated liver function test results, pyuria, and pulmonary edema.

QuestionStem: A 40-year-old female presents for a health maintenance examination. Her medical history is unremarkable, but her family history is significant for colorectal cancer in her father, thyroid cancer in her paternal aunt, and endometrial cancer in her maternal grandmother. The patient says that her friend was diagnosed with breast cancer 2 weeks ago and is BRCA2 positive. She is anxious about her risk of breast cancer and requests a mammogram and BRCA testing. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force guidelines, which one of the following would be most appropriate for this patient regarding genetic testing for BRCA1/2? A) Reassurance only B) Risk assessment using the 7-Question Family History Screening Tool C) Testing for BRCA1/2 mutation D) Genetic counseling
Explanation: For this patient, reassurance only is indicated. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends against routine screening, risk assessment, counseling, and testing in all women for BRCA1/2 mutations (D recommendation). Only women with a personal or family history of breast, ovarian, tubal, or peritoneum cancer or specific susceptibility to BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations should be formally evaluated with a risk assessment tool. Furthermore, genetic testing for BRCA1 and BRCA2 is indicated only after a positive risk assessment score is calculated and subsequent genetic counseling is received.

QuestionStem: You are seeing a patient at 12 weeks' gestation for prenatal care. She has a family history of neural tube defects and is taking 4 mg of high-dose folic acid supplementation daily. Her pregnancy is progressing normally, and she has no concerns. Which one of the following should you recommend for this patient regarding ongoing folic acid supplementation? A) Continuing with 4 mg daily for the remainder of the pregnancy B) Decreasing the dosage to 1 mg daily for the remainder of the pregnancy C) Decreasing the dosage to 0.4 mg daily for the remainder of the pregnancy D) Discontinuing folic acid
Reference: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists’ Committee on Practice Bulletins—Gynecology. ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 194: polycystic ovary syndrome. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131(6):e157-e171. | Pertussis (whooping cough): Clinicians. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, reviewed 2019. | Final Recommendation Statement: Vitamin Supplementation to Prevent Cancer and CVD: Preventive Medication. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2014. | FitzGerald JD, Dalbeth N, Mikuls T, et al: 2020 American College of Rheumatology guideline for the management of gout. Arthritis Care Res (Hoboken) 2020;72(6):744-760. | Grimard BH, Safford RE, Burns EL: Aortic stenosis: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2016;93(5):371-378.
Explanation: Supplementation with folic acid leading up to and during pregnancy can significantly reduce the risk of neural tube defects (NTDs). Ideally, supplementation should be started 1â€“3 months prior to conception. Those with pregnancies at higher risk of NTDs are candidates for high-dose folic acid supplementation, which is more effective at prevention. This patient is considered high risk as she has a family history of NTDs. The recommended dose of folic acid is 4 mg per day until 12 weeks after conception, and then 0.4 mg per day after 12 weeks. The rationale for decreasing the dose after 12 weeks is that organogenesis is largely complete by this stage and there is theoretical concern for fetal toxicity. Folic acid supplementation is recommended in addition to the usual dietary sources of folic acid.

QuestionStem: A 55-year-old male with a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease presents for management of recurrent gout. He has had multiple attacks this year despite dietary changes. His uric acid level is 8.6 mg/dL (N 4.5â€“8.0) and he would like to start gout prophylaxis. Which one of the following would be most appropriate to initiate at this time? A) Allopurinol (Zyloprim) B) Colchicine (Colcrys) C) Febuxostat (Uloric) D) Pegloticase (Krystexxa) E) Allopurinol plus colchicine
Explanation: Gout is a common crystalline arthropathy. Long-term treatment with urate-lowering medications is effective in preventing recurrent attacks and is generally well tolerated. Allopurinol is typically used as first-line treatment for gout in all patients, even in those with kidney disease. The American College of Rheumatology (ACR) strongly recommends that an anti-inflammatory medication, such as colchicine, be given when allopurinol is initiated to prevent an acute flare. The allopurinol dosage should be adjusted to target a uric acid level <6 mg/dL. Colchicine alone is useful in the setting of an acute flare, as are NSAIDs or corticosteroids. Febuxostat is a urate-lowering medication that works by inhibiting xanthine oxidase, which interferes with the conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. While this medication is indicated for chronic gout, it is not a first-line choice. The ACR strongly recommends against pegloticase as a first-line therapy due to cost, lack of evidence regarding safety, and other options with more favorable benefit to harm ratios.

QuestionStem: In a patient with aortic stenosis, which one of the following is an indication for referral for possible valve replacement? A) Calcification of the valve on an echocardiogram B) Development of mitral insufficiency on an echocardiogram C) Left ventricular hypertrophy noted on a chest x-ray D) A systolic ejection murmur that has increased from grade 1/6 to grade 2/6 E) New-onset angina
Explanation: Once symptoms occur in patients with aortic stenosis, the clinical outcome is extremely poor, with 2-year survival rates below 50%. It is well established that this prognosis can be reversed by valve replacement, which has acceptable levels of operative morbidity and mortality, and postoperative survival rates similar to those of age-matched normal adults. Angina could be an indication of the seriousness of aortic valve stenosis. Patients should also be evaluated for coronary artery disease.

QuestionStem: A 23-year-old male presents on a spring day with a 2â€“3-day history of mild nasal congestion, sneezing, and slight watery discharge. He does not have a fever or other significant concerns. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment? A) Intranasal antihistamines B) Intranasal corticosteroids C) Intranasal cromolyn D) Oral antibiotics E) Oral corticosteroids
Explanation: This patient has mild allergic rhinitis. Intranasal corticosteroids are considered first-line therapy. Intranasal or oral antihistamines may be considered given the patient's mild symptoms, but they are less effective. Intranasal cromolyn is less effective and requires multiple dosing. Since there is no apparent infection, oral antibiotics are not indicated. Oral corticosteroids are also not indicated at this time. Some experts recommend nasal saline irrigation to remove allergens from the nares, but this approach has limited evidence and would augment, not replace, pharmacotherapy.

QuestionStem: A 30-year-old female presents for follow-up of infertility. She and her husband have been trying to conceive for the past 12 months. She reports a history of irregular menstrual bleeding over the past several years. A physical examination is unremarkable except for a BMI of 29 kg/m2. You suspect polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and recommend diagnostic testing with fasting laboratory studies scheduled for 8:00 a.m. An elevation in which one of the following hormones would most strongly support a diagnosis of PCOS in this patient? A) 17-Hydroxyprogesterone B) Cortisol C) Glucose D) Insulin E) Testosterone
Reference: Williams T, Moore JB, Regehr J. Polycystic ovary syndrome: common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(3):264-272.
Explanation: While there is no single test to diagnose polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), the diagnosis can be made based on the presence of at least 2 of the 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligoanovulation, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasonography. Other conditions that may mimic PCOS must be excluded, including an androgen-secreting tumor, exogenous androgen exposure, Cushing syndrome, nonclassical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, primary hypothalamic amenorrhea, primary ovarian insufficiency, acromegaly, thyroid disease, prolactin disorders, and genetic disorders that impede insulin action. Hyperandrogenism may be diagnosed clinically with evidence of hirsutism, androgenic alopecia, acne, or seborrhea. Additionally, it may be diagnosed biochemically with elevated androgen levels, such as an elevated free testosterone level. An elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone level would not suggest PCOS, but rather a possible diagnosis of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which may share overlapping clinical features with PCOS. An elevated cortisol level would similarly suggest an etiology other than PCOS, such as Cushing syndrome or adrenal tumor, which may also share overlapping clinical features with PCOS. Although insulin resistance is often part of the complex pathophysiology of PCOS, neither elevated insulin nor glucose levels are part of the Rotterdam criteria for PCOS.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 36-year-old male presents to your clinic with a 36-hour history of dizziness and nausea. He says he feels as though the world is spinning and he tends to fall to the left when he tries to walk. The episodes of dizziness occur spontaneously and can last from minutes to hours. He does not have any recent illness, hearing loss, or tinnitus, and he does not take any medications. An HEENT examination is normal. On neurologic testing, he exhibits horizontal nystagmus with left lateral gaze. Head-impulse testing reveals a rapid movement of both eyes. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Home Epley maneuvers B) Reassurance and antiemetics C) Prednisone, 40 mg daily for 5 days D) Valacyclovir (Valtrex), 1000 mg twice daily for 7 days E) MRI of the brain
Explanation: This patient's vertigo is caused by vestibular neuritis, as evidenced by its spontaneity, rotatory vertigo, nausea, and listing to the affected side with walking. In this scenario, head-impulse testing shows saccade, and the head-impulse, nystagmus, test of skew (HINTS) examination is significant for horizontal nystagmus to the affected side. Vestibular neuritis is most often found in patients 30â€“50 years of age and is thought to have a viral etiology. It involves prolonged, continuous vertigo, while benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is characterized by brief, episodic vertigo associated with head movements. Vestibular neuritis usually resolves on its own within a few days, although some patients have recurrent symptoms for months. The treatment for vestibular neuritis is reassurance. Antiemetics can be used for up to 3 days. Patients with symptoms that last longer should be referred for vestibular rehabilitation. Home Epley maneuvers are appropriate for vertigo caused by BPPV, but are neither indicated nor effective for vestibular neuritis (SOR A). Corticosteroids and antiviral medications have not been shown to be effective in treating vestibular neuritis. MRI of the brain is indicated for patients who exhibit signs of central vertigo or those with hearing loss (SOR C).

QuestionStem: A 24-year-old gravida 2 para 2 presents to your office with melasma that has not resolved since she gave birth 6 months ago. She is eager to be treated. Which one of the following topical treatments would be the most effective for this patient's melasma? A) Azelaic acid B) Hydroquinone C) Imiquimod (Aldara, Zyclara) D) Benzoyl peroxide plus a corticosteroid E) A combination of fluocinolone, hydroquinone, and tretinoin
Explanation: Melasma is a common condition in pregnancy that occurs in up to 75% of patients. It is caused by increased levels of progesterone, estrogen, and melanocyte-stimulating hormone. While it resolves in 90% of cases during the postpartum period, it can persist and may recur in subsequent pregnancies or with the use of oral contraception. Topical tretinoin, hydroquinone, and a corticosteroid such as fluocinolone can all be used in the treatment of melasma, and a combination of the 3 is the most effective. These may be prescribed as a single formulation, which may be cost prohibitive, or as individual components. Azelaic acid and benzoyl peroxide are used to treat acne and are not effective for treating melasma. Imiquimod is an immunomodulator that is helpful in the treatment of condyloma acuminata but has no role in the treatment of melasma.

QuestionStem: You see a newborn for a routine wellness check. The parents tell you that there is a family history of allergies to eggs and peanuts, and they ask how to reduce their child's risk of developing these food allergies. Evidence supports exposing the patient to these potentially allergenic foods starting at which one of the following ages? A) 4â€“6 months B) 9â€“12 months C) 1â€“2 years D) 3â€“5 years E) >5 years
Explanation: The lowest risk of developing future allergies to many foods, including peanuts and eggs, occurs with early exposure at 4â€“6 months of age. Peanut butter can be thinned with water, breast milk, cowâ€™s milk, or formula. Peanuts should not be given in their original hard form because it is a choking hazard. Eggs should be cooked well prior to eating.

QuestionStem: A 23-year-old female presents after a sexual assault that occurred the prior evening. She reports nonconsensual penetrative vaginal intercourse. The assailant was an acquaintance with unknown medical history. The patient takes no medications and is up to date on vaccinations, including tetanus, HPV, and hepatitis B, although her hepatitis B serology status is unknown. She elects to undergo a medical forensic examination, which is completed with the help of a certified Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner. A urine pregnancy test is negative, and sexually transmitted infection testing is performed. Which one of the following would be most appropriate to offer at this time? A) Emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide (Descovy) B) Norgestimate/ethinyl estradiol (Sprintec) C) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) D) Azithromycin (Zithromax), ceftriaxone, and metronidazole (Flagyl) E) The etonogestrel implant (Nexplanon)
Explanation: In addition to trauma-informed evaluation and counseling, women who experience sexual assault should be offered forensic examination, emergency contraception, and screening and prevention for sexually transmitted infections. Empiric treatment for Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, and Trichomonas vaginalis is appropriate and should include ceftriaxone, azithromycin, and either metronidazole or tinidazole. Postexposure prophylaxis for HIV should be made available with triple therapy for 28 days, rather than the double therapy regimens used in pre-exposure prophylaxis. Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir is used to treat hepatitis C infection, but routine postexposure prophylaxis is not recommended. Long-term contraception may be offered, but patients should first be provided with effective emergency contraception, such as ulipristal, levonorgestrel, or the copper IUD.

QuestionStem: A 29-year-old female presents with a long-standing history of episodes of depressed mood, headaches, and irritability that occur in the 1â€“2 weeks preceding her menses. She has a history of anxiety that is well controlled on fluoxetine (Prozac), 60 mg daily. She has a copper IUD (Paragard) for contraception. She has tried combined oral contraceptives to control the premenstrual symptoms, but found that they worsened her irritability. Which one of the following treatments would be most effective for this patient's symptoms? A) Extended-release bupropion, 150 mg daily B) Calcium carbonate, 600 mg twice daily C) Dutasteride (Avodart), 0.5 mg daily D) Vitamin D (cholecalciferol), 1000 IU daily 3 E) Acupuncture
Explanation: Symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) with mood instability can be reduced with 1000â€“1200 mg of calcium carbonate daily (SOR B). Patients with PMS symptoms have been found to have cyclic changes in calcium levels that may account for the symptoms, but the mechanism of action is still unknown. All SSRIs are first-line treatments for symptoms of PMS and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), but bupropion has not been shown to be effective. Continuous combined oral contraceptives are also first-line treatment (SOR A). High-dose dutasteride (2.5 mg daily) has been shown to alleviate PMDD symptoms, but it can have a deleterious effect on mood and is teratogenic. Low-dose dutasteride has not demonstrated beneficial effect on PMDD symptoms. Patients with PMS have not been found to have low levels of vitamin D, and supplementing with vitamin D (cholecalciferol) has not been clearly demonstrated to 3 alleviate PMS symptoms. There is insufficient evidence to prove the benefit of acupuncture in reducing PMS symptoms.

QuestionStem: A 72-year-old female with osteoporosis (T-score â€“2.8) is seen in your office for the first time. She was prescribed alendronate (Fosamax), 70 mg weekly, by her previous physician. The patient took the medication but discontinued it after 4 weeks because of gastrointestinal symptoms. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate action at this time? A) Discontinuing the medication only B) Instructing the patient to take the medication before bed C) Instructing the patient to take the medication with food D) Discontinuing the medication and starting raloxifene (Evista) E) Discontinuing the medication and arranging for intravenous bisphosphonates
Reference: Hartman-Adams H, Gerbo RM, George S. Tuberculosis: common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(3):308-315.
Explanation: Women â‰¥65 years of age should be tested for osteoporosis. A bone mineral density score â‰¤â€“2.5 indicates the need for treatment, as seen in this patient. Oral bisphosphonates, such as alendronate, are considered first-line treatment. To minimize gastrointestinal irritation, oral bisphosphonates should be taken on an empty stomach with 8 oz of water, and the patient should remain upright and avoid food and beverages other than plain water for at least 30 minutes afterward. If oral bisphosphonates are not tolerated despite correct administration, intravenous bisphosphonates should be considered. In this case, the patient should discontinue alendronate and transition to intravenous bisphosphonate therapy. Raloxifene is not the preferred initial treatment.

QuestionStem: A 27-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with a fever, headache, and stiff neck. You diagnose sepsis and perform a lumbar puncture. The results are consistent with bacterial meningitis. In addition to antibiotics, which one of the following intravenous therapies is most likely to improve mortality? A) Acyclovir B) Dexamethasone C) Furosemide D) Glycerol E) Immunoglobulin
Explanation: Despite optimal therapy, the mortality and loss of quality-adjusted life-years from bacterial meningitis is significant. Empiric therapy for adults includes vancomycin and ceftriaxone. Intravenous (IV) dexamethasone, when given shortly before or at the time of administration of IV antibiotics, has been shown to improve mortality (odds ratio 0.90) and neurologic sequelae. Bactericidal antibiotics help to reduce inflammation. Acyclovir would be indicated if there is clinical concern for herpes simplex virus or symptoms of encephalitis. While optimal fluid management for meningitis should be tailored based on clinical evaluation, this patient has also been diagnosed with sepsis and should receive appropriate fluid resuscitation; therefore, IV furosemide would be inappropriate. Glycerol is a hyperosmolar agent intended to reduce intracranial pressure in bacterial meningitis, but it cannot be recommended for treatment due to an increased risk of death according to available data in adults with a high prevalence of HIV. IV immunoglobulin is not a recognized treatment for bacterial meningitis.

QuestionStem: A 33-year-old male presents for follow-up of long-standing heartburn. His symptoms have been present for 3 years and recent treatment for 1 month with pantoprazole (Protonix) has only provided partial relief. He has a history of allergic rhinitis that is treated with fluticasone propionate nasal spray (Flonase). He has had dysphagia with solids, and a food impaction 3 months ago that resolved spontaneously. Upper endoscopy reveals eosinophilic esophagitis. Which one of the following is most likely to improve this patientâ€™s symptoms? A) A clotrimazole oropharyngeal lozenge B) Budesonide oral suspension (Eohilia) C) Oral famotidine (Pepcid) D) Oral metoclopramide (Reglan) E) Oral montelukast (Singulair)
Explanation: Eosinophilic esophagitis is an inflammatory condition triggered by food antigens in sensitive individuals. Endoscopic findings of linear grooves, concentric rings, and whitish exudates may suggest its presence, but the diagnosis is confirmed histologically based on the presence of >15 eosinophils/hpf and the exclusion of other causes of eosinophilia. The diagnosis should be suspected in patients with heartburn who have atopic illnesses such as allergic rhinitis, asthma, or eczema and who do not respond to proton pump inhibitor therapy. Eosinophilic esophagitis is more common in men in their 20s and 30s, and dysphagia may be present for 4 or more years before diagnosis. More than half of patients with this condition may have a history of food impaction. First-line pharmacologic treatment is oral budesonide. Alternatively, inhalant corticosteroids may be sprayed into the oral cavity during a breath hold and then swallowed to minimize pulmonary deposition. Treatment with proton pump inhibitors may help, but they do not provide complete relief in two-thirds of cases. Dietary changes can be very helpful, but consultation with an allergist may be needed. Immunosuppressed individuals such as patients with HIV or diabetes mellitus may have Candida esophagitis, which is treated with fluconazole rather than clotrimazole. Adding an H -blocker such as famotidine would help in cases of erosive esophagitis. Metoclopramide 2 was previously used to treat severe GERD, but it is no longer encouraged due to side effects. While montelukast is used to treat asthma and allergic rhinitis, it is not as effective for eosinophilic esophagitis.

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old male with a recent diagnosis of Crohn disease presents to your office for a health maintenance examination. His gastroenterologist advised that he ask you about vaccine updates. He has not seen a primary care physician in over 15 years. His current medications include methotrexate and azathioprine (Imuran). He received a Tdap vaccine 6 years ago at an urgent care clinic and recently received a seasonal influenza vaccine and an updated COVID-19 vaccine at his gastroenterologistâ€™s office. He is confident that he received all of his childhood vaccines. Which one of the following vaccines should you recommend today? A) 20-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV20, Prevnar 20) B) Meningococcal C) MMR D) Tdap E) Zoster vaccine (Shingrix)
Explanation: Adults with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) should receive a pneumococcal vaccine. Meningococcal vaccine is currently recommended for adolescents with IBD. The MMR vaccine is contraindicated in patients already on immunosuppressive treatment. Because this patient's Tdap vaccine was administered <10 years ago, he is not due for another dose. Similarly, his next dose of zoster vaccine will be due when he is 50 years of age, and there is no recommendation to administer the next vaccine before the age of 50 in patients with IBD.

QuestionStem: A 19-year-old college student with a recent history of sinusitis and a past medical history of depression, adrenal insufficiency, and hypothyroidism is brought to the urgent care clinic by her roommates after she experienced a syncopal episode. The patient reports progressive weakness and fatigue, increasingly severe abdominal pain, and repeated episodes of nausea and vomiting over the last few days. A review of her electronic health record shows the following medications: Amoxicillin/clavulanate (Augmentin) Escitalopram (Lexapro) Fludrocortisone Levothyroxine (Synthroid) Prednisolone Her vital signs at presentation include a blood pressure of 97/58 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 116 beats/min, and a temperature of 37.2 Â°C (99.0 Â°F). A basic metabolic panel is notable for the following results: Sodium 133 mEq/L (N 136â€“145) Potassium 5.8 mEq/L (N 3.4â€“4.4) Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (N 0.6â€“1.1) Glucose 60 mg/dL Which one of the following therapies should be started as soon as possible to prevent further worsening of this patient's acute condition? A) Intramuscular glucagon, 1 mg B) Intravenous dexamethasone, 1 mg C) Intravenous hydrocortisone (Solu-Cortef), 100 mg D) Intravenous levofloxacin, 500 mg E) Intravenous ondansetron, 4 mg
Reference: Michels A, Michels N: Addison disease: Early detection and treatment principles. Am Fam Physician 2014;89(7):563-568. | Bornstein SR, Allolio B, Arlt W, et al: Diagnosis and treatment of primary adrenal insufficiency: An Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2016;101(2):364-389. | Runser LA, Gauer RL, Houser A: Syncope: Evaluation and differential diagnosis. Am Fam Physician 2017;95(5):303-312. | Westerfield KL, Koenig K, Oh R: Breastfeeding: Common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician 2018;98(6):368-373.
Explanation: This patient's chronic corticosteroid use, symptoms, and electrolyte abnormalities suggest she is experiencing an adrenal crisis; the correct initial treatment of this is intravenous hydrocortisone. Acute illness, dehydration, severe physical stress, or nonadherence to treatment are all possible causes of acute adrenal crisis in patients with chronic adrenal insufficiency. These patients may present with weakness, severe fatigue, abdominal pain, tachycardia, and hypotension. Laboratory abnormalities can include hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and, in some cases, hypoglycemia. If left untreated, adrenal crisis can be fatal. Glucagon is primarily used to treat hypoglycemia in patients with insulin-dependent diabetes, but it will not correct the cardiovascular abnormalities of adrenal crisis. Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid that can be used to treat adrenal crisis, but it is not the preferred therapy according to Endocrine Society practice guidelines. Additionally, a 1-mg dose is subtherapeutic for adrenal crisis. Dexamethasone is sometimes used at this dose to test for primary hyperadrenalism. Levofloxacin is not appropriate for treatment of this patient's adrenal crisis. Although ondansetron may help reduce vomiting, it will not correct the underlying cause of this patient's illness.

QuestionStem: A 37-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after collapsing while standing in line at a bookstore. Her friend witnessed the episode and describes it as "fainting." A history and physical examination were performed at the bedside while triaging the patient. The patient's past medical history and a physical examination, including vital signs and a neurologic assessment, are unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient with suspected syncope? A) No further testing B) An EKG C) An EEG D) Carotid ultrasonography E) Head CT
Explanation: Electrocardiography (EKG) is a primary evaluation tool, along with a comprehensive history and physical examination, to investigate the etiology of a syncopal episode (SOR C). Electroencephalography (EEG) is not recommended as a routine test. Carotid ultrasonography is not recommended for evaluation of syncope in patients with a normal physical examination (SOR B). Similarly, head CT and MRI are not recommended as neurologic imaging studies in patients with a normal neurologic examination after a syncopal episode (SOR B). If the patient were noted to experience syncope with exertion, an exercise stress test would be appropriate (SOR C). However, this patient was observed to be at rest when the "fainting" occurred.

QuestionStem: A 64-year-old male presents with urinary frequency. His symptoms have been ongoing for months, but he says they have acutely worsened in the last 6â€“8 weeks. His symptoms are predominantly storage-related with daytime frequency, nocturia, urgency, and even infrequent incontinence. He also reports difficulty voiding, noting a weak stream and hesitancy. He does not have erectile dysfunction. Urinalysis is negative. In addition to behavioral and lifestyle alterations, which one of the following medications should you recommend for initial therapy? A) A 5-Î±-reductase inhibitor B) An Î±-antagonist C) An anticholinergic D) A Î² -agonist 3 E) A phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor
Explanation: Men with lower urinary tract symptoms may have overactive bladder, bladder outlet obstruction, or both. Differentiating overactive bladder and bladder outlet obstruction can be difficult due to significant symptom overlap. Overactive bladder is most often due to detrusor overactivity, and bladder outlet obstruction is most often secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia. The most appropriate initial treatment for these symptoms is an Î±- blocker, such as tamsulosin, alfuzosin, or terazosin (Evidence Level A), regardless of the predominance of storage symptoms (eg, daytime frequency, nocturia, urgency, incontinence) or voiding symptoms (eg, weak stream, hesitancy, straining, dribbling). The American Urological Association recommends 5-Î±-reductase inhibitors if prostate volume is â‰¥30 mL on imaging, the prostate is enlarged on examination, or PSA levels exceed 1.5 ng/mL. 5-Î±-Reductase inhibitors can take up to a year to take full effect. Anticholinergics (oxybutynin) and Î² -agonists (mirabegron) may be used alone or in 3 combination with Î±-blockers for patients with predominantly storage-related symptoms if Î±-blockers are not adequately effective. Some guidelines recommend assessing postvoid residual volume before initiating these agents. A daily phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor (typically tadalafil) can be used as initial monotherapy or as combination therapy in patients with erectile dysfunction, although it is not generally recommended as first-line treatment. The mechanism of action is not fully understood.

QuestionStem: A 5-day-old male is brought to your office by his mother for an initial well child visit. After an uncomplicated, full-term pregnancy, the mother gave birth 102 hours ago through vaginal delivery and was discharged 48 hours ago. She shares that this is her first child, and she is concerned that he is not getting enough milk despite exclusively breastfeeding 10â€“12 times per day. Which one of the following factors, if present, would indicate inadequate breastfeeding at this time? A) Four green stools per day B) Four wet diapers per day C) Infant weight loss of 5% from birth weight D) Maternal breasts that appear small and not full E) Maternal nipple pain during the first 15 seconds of each feed
Explanation: Recognition of adequate newborn feeding and parental lactation counseling is important in the care of mothers and infants. An infant who is feeding adequately should produce 6 or more wet diapers and 3 or more stools after 4 days of life. Infant weight loss is normal in the first 3 days of life, but a weight loss of more than 7% from birth weight or ongoing weight loss after 3 days of life should raise concern. Maternal breasts that appear smaller or less full do not necessarily indicate poor feeding; however, lack of any breast changes or leaking of milk by day 5 post partum may indicate a problem with milk supply. Maternal nipple pain is normal with breastfeeding, but pain that persists beyond the first 30â€“60 seconds should prompt evaluation and counseling. If supplementation with formula is deemed medically necessary, protocol from the Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine should be followed.

QuestionStem: A 25-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with a 4-day history of fever, chills, myalgias, and cough. He lives with a roommate and works remotely from home. His last influenza vaccine was 5 years ago, and his most recent COVID-19 vaccine was 2 years ago. His temperature is 38.3 Â°C (100.9 Â°F), and his heart rate and blood pressure are within the normal range. A physical examination is normal except for occasional rhonchi. Rapid antigen testing is positive for influenza B and negative for influenza A and COVID-19. Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's influenza B? A) Supportive therapy B) Methylprednisolone (Medrol) C) Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) D) Rimantadine
Reference: Gaitonde DY, Moore FC, Morgan MK: Influenza: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2019;100(12):751-758.
Explanation: According to the CDC, antiviral treatment for influenza is not recommended for nonâ€“high- risk individuals who present more than 48 hours after symptom onset. Thus, supportive therapy is most important for this patient who is at low risk for hospitalization and death. Methylprednisolone is not recommended to treat influenza. The risk for hospitalization and death is not reduced in adults and children treated with a neuraminidase inhibitor in the outpatient setting, as supported by systematic and unpublished randomized clinical trials. While oseltamivir is recommended as first-line therapy for high-risk patients, it is not indicated for this patient, who has no underlying medical conditions or known exposure to high-risk individuals and presents more than 48 hours after symptom onset. Rimantadine is not active against influenza B and is also not considered first-line medical therapy for influenza.

QuestionStem: A 30-year-old male with a long-standing history of obsessive-compulsive disorder presents for follow-up to discuss potential pharmacotherapy due to an inadequate treatment response to psychotherapy. Which one of the following medications would be most likely to improve this patientâ€™s symptoms while minimizing the risk of adverse side effects? A) Bupropion B) Buspirone C) Clomipramine (Anafranil) D) Quetiapine (Seroquel) E) Sertraline (Zoloft)
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Falls Prevention in Community-Dwelling Older Adults: Interventions. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2018. | Learn the signs. Act early: milestones. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Reviewed June 6, 2023. | Chey WD, Leontiadis GI, Howden CW, Moss SF. ACG clinical guideline: treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection. Am J Gastroenterol. 2017;112(2):212-239. | Lanham JS, Nelson NK, Hendren B, Jordan TS: Outpatient burn care: Prevention and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2020;101(8):463-470. | Stevens SM, Woller SC, Kreuziger LB, et al. Antithrombotic therapy for VTE disease: second update of the CHEST guideline and expert panel report. Chest. 2021;160(6):e545-e608.
Explanation: The most effective medication therapy options for obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) are SSRIs. Fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline, and paroxetine all have approval from the FDA for the treatment of OCD. Dosages that are higher than usual are often required to achieve symptom control. Clomipramine, a tricyclic antidepressant, has an indication from the FDA for treatment of OCD, but a higher toxicity risk makes it less favorable for first-line treatment. Bupropion (a norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor), buspirone (an atypical anxiolytic), and quetiapine (an atypical antipsychotic) are not first-line treatment for OCD.

QuestionStem: A 76-year-old female with hypertension sees you for a routine follow-up visit. She fell about 2 weeks ago while hurrying to answer her home telephone, and sustained bruising and swelling of the left lower leg. She does not recall feeling dizzy or lightheaded before the fall, and she did not hit her head or lose consciousness. She was able to get up and walk afterward without any limitations but developed a large bruise over the lateral hip. She has not had any previous falls and she feels well today. She lives alone and performs activities of daily living independently. Which one of the following has the best evidence to prevent future falls and associated serious injury? A) Patient education on fall harms and prevention B) Use of a cane while walking C) Referral to a group exercise program, including gait, balance, and functional training D) Referral to an ophthalmologist for a comprehensive vision assessment E) Referral to a podiatrist for footwear assessment
Explanation: Falls are a common cause of serious injury and death in older adults in the United States. A 2018 study found that 27.5% of adults â‰¥65 years of age had fallen at least once in the past year (714 falls per 1000 community-dwelling older adults). More than 300,000 older adults are hospitalized each year for hip fracture, which is one of the most serious potential complications from falls in older adults. Falls are also the leading cause of injury-related death in older adults. A history of a fall is a key risk factor for future falls. Other risks include sensory and cognitive deficits, acute and chronic health conditions, alcohol and drug use, and certain other medication use. In community-dwelling older adults who are identified as being at higher risk of falls, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force found that exercise interventions lower the risk of falls and should be recommended. Both individual and group exercise programs are beneficial and there is no data to recommend any single type of exercise over others. Multifactorial interventions to reduce falls typically include an assessment of modifiable risk factors followed by a customized approach to reducing risk. Evaluations may include balance, gait, vision, cognition, postural blood pressure, medication use, and the home environment. This assessment differs from a focused diagnostic assessment of a patient with a specific concern such as vision change, dizziness, or numbness in the feet. Interventions to address risk factors identified in a multifactorial screening program may offer benefit to high-risk individuals, although studies have found a small net benefit on average. A 2023 Cochrane review comprising 22 studies on environmental interventions found evidence of benefit for reducing fall hazards at home by using a validated tool to identify and address hazards, usually via a home visit. Patient education, assistive devices, vision impairment correction, and footwear selection were not found to be beneficial.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following would be most appropriate for initial treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection? A) Dual therapy with vonoprazan (Voquezna) and amoxicillin B) Triple therapy with omeprazole, amoxicillin, and clarithromycin C) Triple therapy with omeprazole, amoxicillin, and levofloxacin D) Quadruple therapy with bismuth, omeprazole, metronidazole, and tetracycline
Explanation: Infection with Helicobacter pylori should be treated when it causes symptoms. Several regimens exist for the treatment of H pylori infection. Guidelines currently recommend optimized quadruple therapy with bismuth, metronidazole, omeprazole, and tetracycline. This combination is approximately 91% effective at eradicating H pylori infection. Dual therapy with vonoprazan and amoxicillin is an option for patients who are unable or unwilling to take quadruple therapy, but its eradiation rates only range from 70% to 80%. Regimens containing clarithromycin or levofloxacin should be limited to infections with documented susceptibility to macrolides or fluoroquinolones, respectively.

QuestionStem: The parent of a 5-year-old male calls your office after the child sustains burns to his arms and chest while helping his father boil pasta in the kitchen. The father tells you that the burned areas of skin are red without any open areas. He estimates the size of the burns on the arms at 2â€“3 cm in diameter and those on the chest at 5â€“6 cm. The child is crying in pain and the father asks what he should do. Which one of the following immediate interventions has the best evidence of reducing burn depth, improving healing time, and decreasing skin graft requirements? A) Cooling the burn surfaces with running tap water B) Applying ice to the burn surfaces C) Covering the burn surfaces with cool, wet gauze D) Applying aloe vera gel to the burn surfaces and leaving them uncovered E) Applying diclofenac gel to the burn surfaces and leaving them uncovered
Explanation: Burns are a common cause of injury in the United States, but most are minor and can be treated in the outpatient setting. The initial treatment goals for burn injuries include limiting the extent of the injury, cleaning the area, and managing pain. Removing clothing limits the injury related to scalding. The most effective way to limit the extent of injury is to cool the burn surface by running cool tap water over the burn surface for at least 20 minutes. This intervention has been shown to reduce burn depth, improve healing time, and decrease skin graft requirements. Ideally, burns should be cooled within the first 30 minutes of injury, but some evidence suggests benefit up to 3 hours after the initial injury. Applying ice to the burn surfaces is not recommended as a cooling strategy due to the risk of vasoconstriction, which can contribute to tissue damage. Wet dressings are not effective as a cooling strategy and are not recommended. For superficial burns such as those described in this patient, aloe vera and NSAIDs can be used to address pain after the initial cooling strategy, but these are not the recommended first step. Burns are classified by their depth: superficial (first degree), partial thickness (second degree), and full thickness (third degree). Superficial burns are limited to the epidermal layer. They are painful and appear red (blanchable), with intact skin and no blistering. Partial-thickness burns are deeper and involve injury to the dermis. Superficial partial- thickness burns may blister and be exudative, whereas deep partial-thickness burns are drier and do not blanch. Full-thickness burns involve the entire dermis and extend into the fat and connective tissue layers. They appear white rather than red, and are dry with a waxy appearance. Patients often experience less pain due to the destruction of nerve endings. Deep partial-thickness and full-thickness burns should be evaluated and treated at a burn center.

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old female with a past medical history of obesity and cigarette smoking presents with a popliteal deep vein thrombosis of the right leg, confirmed on ultrasonography. She has no prior history of venous thromboembolism and has not had any recent surgery, trauma, or immobilization. She does not take any medications. Which one of the following anticoagulation regimens would be the most appropriate initial treatment? A) A direct-acting oral anticoagulant (DOAC) only B) Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) only C) Warfarin only D) LMWH for 7 days, followed by warfarin E) Unfractionated heparin for 48 hours, followed by a DOAC
Explanation: Direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are now recommended as monotherapy for outpatient management of deep vein thrombosis. DOACs reach therapeutic levels quickly; therefore, concomitant initiation of unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is not necessary. DOACs have less major bleeding and require less monitoring, making them preferred over warfarin. Previously, unfractionated heparin was administered during hospitalization while warfarin was simultaneously initiated; however, subcutaneous LMWH can now be started with warfarin as an outpatient and continued until the goal INR is reached if a DOAC is unaffordable.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following puts a patient at the highest risk of developing breast cancer? A) A first pregnancy at <30 years of age B) Onset of menopause at <50 years of age C) Fatty breasts on mammography D) A grandmother with a history of breast cancer E) Chest radiation therapy as a child
Reference: Chang K, Guarderas JC: Allergy testing: Common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician 2018;98(1):34-39.
Explanation: Women who received chest radiotherapy as children are at increased risk for breast cancer as adults, with those who received whole lung radiation at especially high risk. Other risk factors for breast cancer include the onset of menopause at >55 years of age, a first pregnancy at >35 years of age, and a first-degree relative with breast cancer. Patients with dense breasts have a higher amount of glandular and stromal tissue compared with adipose tissue on mammography and are at higher risk for breast cancer.

QuestionStem: An 83-year-old female sees you for follow-up 2 weeks after being discharged home from an acute rehabilitation facility. She experienced an acute ischemic stroke 6 weeks ago and spent several weeks in the rehabilitation setting after an initial hospital stay. Home health services have been assisting her since she returned home. Today she reports improved mobility, speech, and swallowing, but persistent difficulties with low mood. She finds it difficult to enjoy hobbies and avoids visiting with family and friends. She has been sleeping more than usual and finds it difficult to get out of bed in the morning. You diagnose poststroke depression. In addition to talk therapy, which one of the following is recommended as first-line therapy for this condition? A) Bupropion B) Escitalopram (Lexapro) C) Nortriptyline (Pamelor) D) Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation
Explanation: Poststroke depression (PSD) is a common complication that occurs in 20%â€“60% of patients with stroke, depending on the definition used in diagnosis and the timing of symptom onset. The exact pathophysiology of PSD is unknown but appears complex and multifactorial. The incidence of PSD varies with the location of the stroke. Abnormal neurotransmitter and inflammatory marker levels have been associated with PSD. SSRIs such as escitalopram are considered first-line therapy for PSD. Other options include SNRIs, tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), and newer multimodal agents such as vortioxetine. Bupropion may be considered if SSRIs are ineffective or cannot be tolerated. As a class, TCAs have a higher anticholinergic effect than SSRIs and are therefore not preferred, although nortriptyline has been evaluated in randomized, controlled trials and found to be effective in treating PSD. There is a growing body of evidence to support the use of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation, but it is not recommended as first-line treatment at this time.

QuestionStem: Patients with which one of the following should be provided opioid overdose education and a prescription for naloxone? A) A history of substance use disorder B) A prescription for alprazolam (Xanax), 1 mg nightly as needed C) A 3-day prescription for hydrocodone/acetaminophen, 5/325 mg twice daily D) A prescription through hospice treatment for morphine, 10 mg as needed
Reference: Banerjee ES, Schroeder R, Harrison TD. Metabolic surgery for adult obesity: common questions and answers. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(6):593-601. | Final Recommendation Statement: Perinatal Depression: Preventive Interventions. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2019. | Gentry S, Gentry B: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: Diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician 2017;95(7):433-441. | Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline for the management of heart failure: a report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association joint committee on clinical practice guidelines. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032. | Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016. | Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016. | American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee: 9. Pharmacologic approaches to glycemic treatment: Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2022. Diabetes Care 2022;45(Suppl 1):S125-S143.
Explanation: The 2022 CDC clinical practice guideline for prescribing opioids recommends that clinicians offer naloxone to patients at increased risk of overdose, including patients with a history of substance use, overdose, and sleep-disordered breathing, as well as patients who take higher dosages of opioids (â‰¥50 MME/day), take benzodiazepines with opioids, or are at risk of returning to higher opioid dosages with loss of tolerance, such as when tapering opioid dosages.

QuestionStem: A 55-year-old female with a medical history of hypothyroidism, hypertension, and morbid obesity with a recent gastric bypass surgery presents with a history of dizziness, sweating, shaking, fatigue, and occasional fainting, occurring 1â€“2 hours after consuming beverages containing dairy products or a high sugar content. Her blood glucose levels, measured with her husband's glucometer during each episode, range between 50 mg/dL and 55 mg/dL. Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms? A) Gastroparesis B) Idiopathic postprandial syndrome C) Insulinoma D) Late dumping syndrome
Explanation: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is late dumping syndrome. Dumping syndrome is a common postbariatric surgery complication occurring in about 40% of patients. There are 2 types of dumping syndrome: early dumping syndrome and late dumping syndrome. Early dumping syndrome is caused by the rapid passage of nutrients into the small bowel, resulting in intestinal hormonal release and fluid shift. Symptoms occur within 30â€“60 minutes of eating and include abdominal pain, diarrhea, bloating, nausea, flushing, palpitations, sweating, tachycardia, hypotension, and syncope without chemical evidence of hypoglycemia. Late dumping syndrome is caused by the rapid absorption of carbohydrates, leading to exaggerated insulin secretion, resulting in chemical evidence of hypoglycemia occurring 1â€“3 hours after eating. Gastroparesis, unlike dumping syndrome, results from significantly delayed gastric emptying without hypoglycemia, often due to complications of diabetes mellitus. Idiopathic postprandial syndrome describes hypoglycemic-like symptoms that occur after eating without chemical evidence of hypoglycemia. Insulinoma is a rare insulin-producing tumor of the pancreas that causes hypoglycemia regardless of food intake timing.

QuestionStem: A 10-month-old male is brought to your office with a 1-day history of fussiness and tugging at his ear. The child's mother says that he has had a fever this morning. He is up to date on all immunizations, and he does not have a history of previous ear infections or recent illness. He has a history of hives after being given amoxicillin. On examination the child has a temperature of 39.2 Â°C (102.6 Â°F) but seems to be only slightly uncomfortable. When you examine his ears you note moderate bulging of the left tympanic membrane and normal findings in the right ear. All other findings are normal. According to the American Academy of Pediatrics guidelines, which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial management? A) Amoxicillin B) Amoxicillin/clavulanate (Augmentin) C) Cefdinir D) Ciprofloxacin (Cipro) E) Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim
Explanation: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends antibiotic therapy for children â‰¥6 months of age with severe signs and symptoms of acute otitis media (AOM), including moderate or severe otalgia, otalgia lasting longer than 48 hours, or a temperature â‰¥39 Â°C (102 Â°F), whether the AOM is unilateral or bilateral (SOR B). Children younger than 24 months without severe symptoms should receive antibiotic therapy for bilateral AOM, whereas older children or those with unilateral AOM can be offered the option of observation and follow-up. The usual treatment for AOM is amoxicillin, but an antibiotic with additional Î²-lactamase coverage, such as amoxicillin/clavulanate, should be given if the child has received amoxicillin within the past 30 days, has concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, or has a history of AOM unresponsive to amoxicillin (SOR C). Penicillin-allergic patients should be treated with an alternative antibiotic such as cefdinir, cefuroxime, cefpodoxime, or ceftriaxone. Ciprofloxacin should not be used in children. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is no longer effective for the treatment of acute otitis media due to evidence of Streptococcus pneumoniae resistance.

QuestionStem: A 5-year-old male is brought to the emergency department (ED) by his parents for evaluation after falling from a kitchen table just prior to arrival. The mother states that the child climbed onto the table and fell off before she could reach him. He landed directly on the right side of his head and did not lose consciousness. She witnessed 1 episode of vomiting after the fall but no additional episodes since then. On physical examination, the patient is tearful but consolable. You note a right-sided parietal hematoma. There are no palpable skull fractures or signs of altered mental status. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management? A) Providing reassurance and recommending outpatient follow-up B) Observation in the ED for worsening symptoms C) Radiography of the skull D) CT of the head and neck E) Contacting child protective services
Explanation: This patient likely has a mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI), or concussion, based on the mechanism of injury and the presence of vomiting. This patient's TBI would be classified as mild due to the lack of worsening symptoms or additional concerning findings on examination, such as the child being fussier or holding his head. For these reasons, reassurance and outpatient observation with follow-up is the most appropriate next step. Routine imaging is not recommended for mTBI in children. There is no role for radiography of the skull in the management of mTBI or concussion. CT of the head or neck would only be indicated if the patient were experiencing acutely worsening symptoms, reporting or showing signs of headache, or had a suspected head or neck injury. There is no need to contact child protective services because there has been no delay in seeking care or the presence of other unusual physical findings or behaviors between the parents and child.

QuestionStem: A 32-year-old male presents for advice on smoking cessation. He has smoked 10 cigarettes per day for the past 10 years. His grandmother was recently diagnosed with lung cancer, and he is motivated to quit. You advise him that smoking cessation is the most important action that he can take for his health, and you counsel him about effective behavioral interventions. Which one of the following behavioral changes would improve this patient's chances of smoking cessation? A) Creating a smoke-free home B) Increasing his caffeine intake C) Switching to menthol cigarettes D) Undergoing hypnotherapy
Explanation: Behavioral interventions are effective treatments for smoking cessation. Advising a patient who wishes to stop smoking to create a smoke-free home and car environment is a highly effective behavioral intervention. This advice is an important aspect of a brief 3-step tobacco cessation intervention model (Ask, Advise, Assist/Connect). Caffeine intake does not play a role in smoking cessation. Switching to menthol cigarettes does not have a role in supporting smoking cessation. There is no high- or moderate-quality evidence to support hypnotherapy or acupuncture for smoking cessation. In addition to behavioral interventions, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force also recommends providing FDA-approved pharmacotherapy such as nicotine replacement therapy, sustained-release bupropion hydrochloride, or varenicline. Combined interventions result in increased tobacco-cessation rates.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following medications is the recommended initial management of COPD in patients who are classified as GOLD group A? A) Inhaled umeclidinium (Incruse Ellipta) B) Inhaled aclidinium bromide/formoterol (Duaklir Pressair) C) Inhaled budesonide/formoterol (Symbicort) D) Inhaled fluticasone furoate/umeclidinium/vilanterol (Trelegy Ellipta) E) Nebulized formoterol (Perforomist)
Explanation: Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMAs) such as umeclidinium are recommended as the preferred initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group A. Long-acting Î²-agonists (LABAs) such as formoterol may be used as an alternative for these patients. LAMA/LABA combinations such as aclidinium bromide/formoterol are recommended as initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group B. LAMA/LABA combinations are also recommended for management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group E. Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)/LAMA/LABA combinations such as fluticasone furoate/umeclidinium/vilanterol can also be considered for initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group E, especially if blood eosinophil levels are â‰¥300 cells/Î¼L.

QuestionStem: A 29-year-old female with type 2 diabetes is brought to the emergency department by her family, who state that she has grown increasingly confused over the last few days, with additional symptoms of polyuria and polydipsia. Her initial vital signs demonstrate hypotension and tachycardia. Laboratory Findings Serum glucose 750 mg/dL Serum osmolality 350 mOsm/kg H O (N 275â€“295) 2 Urine ketones trace Arterial pH 7.3 Anion gap normal Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial treatment? A) Subcutaneous short-acting insulin B) Subcutaneous long-acting insulin C) Intravenous isotonic fluid hydration D) Intravenous isotonic fluid hydration with sodium bicarbonate
Explanation: This patient presents with acute confusion, dehydration, hyperglycemia, and hyperosmolality.
