The appropriate diagnosis is hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS), rather than diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), given her lack of significant ketonuria and acidosis. She is hypovolemic and dehydrated as evidenced by her change in vital signs. Initial treatment consists of intravenous fluids with isotonic solutions, such as 0.9% normal saline or lactated Ringer solution. She will also need an intravenous insulin infusion of short-acting insulin at a dose of 0.05 units/kg/hr. After the initial fluid bolus, potassium chloride can be added to intravenous fluids to correct her total body potassium depletion. Sodium bicarbonate should only be considered if the arterial pH is <7.0 (see Figure 4 of the American Diabetes Association guideline for treatment pathways of DKA and HHS).

QuestionStem: A 65-year-old male with symptomatic heart failure presents for follow-up. He currently has no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, but has moderate to significant limitations with ordinary activity. His most recent echocardiogram showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38%. His last hemoglobin A was the highest it has ever been at 6.3%, and he does not take any 1c glucose-lowering medications. His current medications include losartan (Cozaar), carvedilol (Coreg), furosemide (Lasix), and spironolactone (Aldactone). On examination he has a blood pressure of 120/75 mm Hg and an oxygen saturation of 94% on room air, and there are no pulmonary crackles or peripheral edema. Adding which one of the following would be most likely to further reduce this patientâ€™s mortality risk? A) A calcium channel blocker B) A GLP-1 receptor agonist C) An SGLT2 inhibitor D) A thiazide diuretic E) Metformin
Explanation: Treatment with an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended to decrease all-cause mortality in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), regardless of the presence or absence of diabetes mellitus. According to guidelines from the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association, first-line medical management for symptomatic HFrEF is angiotensin receptor/neprilysin inhibitor (ARNi) therapy. If ARNi therapy is not feasible, an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker is second-line therapy. These patients should also receive therapy with a cardioselective Î²-blocker, a loop diuretic (for pulmonary congestion or edema), and a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist. Neither calcium channel blockers (CCBs) nor thiazide diuretics provide mortality benefits in HFrEF; although thiazide diuretics may be used for the adjunctive management of symptomatic congestion and edema, CCBs may lead to worse cardiac outcomes. The potential mortality benefits of GLP-1 receptor agonists in patients with HFrEF are still being investigated. Metformin has been shown to improve mortality in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (left ventricular ejection fraction >50%).

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old male with a past medical history of hyperlipidemia and obesity presents with concerns of polydipsia and polyuria that have progressively worsened over the last 2 weeks. His older sister was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes and your patient asks to be tested. Which one of the following test results would be sufficient to diagnose diabetes in this patient? A) A hemoglobin A â‰¥6.0% 1c B) A 1-hour plasma glucose level â‰¥140 mg/dL on 50-g oral glucose testing C) A fasting plasma glucose level â‰¥120 mg/dL D) A random blood glucose level â‰¥200 mg/dL
Explanation: According to guidelines from the American Diabetes Association, diabetes mellitus can be diagnosed with a random blood glucose level â‰¥200 mg/dL in patients with classic symptoms of hyperglycemia, such as polyuria and polydipsia, as seen in this patient. An 8- hour fasting plasma glucose level â‰¥126 mg/dL, a hemoglobin A â‰¥6.5%, or a 2-hour plasma 1c glucose level â‰¥200 mg/dL on 75-g oral glucose tolerance testing can also indicate diabetes but would require confirmatory testing with a second abnormal laboratory value to confirm the diagnosis. A fasting plasma glucose level 100â€“125 mg/dL or a hemoglobin A 5.7%â€“6.4% suggests a 1c diagnosis of prediabetes. A 1-hour plasma glucose level â‰¥140 mg/dL on 50-g oral glucose testing is an abnormal first step in a 2-step screening approach for gestational diabetes mellitus in pregnant individuals.

QuestionStem: A 36-year-old male presents to your office with continued pain in his right arm after sustaining a right forearm fracture 6 weeks ago that was treated with cast immobilization. At the time of the injury, he noted a severe, throbbing pain in his right forearm that improved with NSAID therapy. Now, he feels a severe, sharp pain with burning and numbness throughout the arm and hand that worsens with any pressure, including clothing. He reports weakness in his right hand and notes that he drops objects such as cups. He is afebrile and his vital signs are within normal limits. An examination reveals pain to light touch in the right arm. The right arm is warmer to the touch than the left arm with trace edema noted. The patient's right-hand grip is 4/5, and his left-hand grip is 5/5. You note 2+ radial and brachial pulses and normal bilateral reflexes. Which one of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's pain? A) Cellulitis B) Complex regional pain syndrome C) Factitious disorder D) Functional neurologic symptom disorder (conversion disorder) E) Peripheral neuropathy
Reference: Lloyd ECO, Dempsey B, Romero L: Complex regional pain syndrome. Am Fam Physician 2021;104(1):49-55.
Explanation: This patient meets the Budapest criteria for complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) including hyperalgesia, temperature asymmetry, evidence of edema, and motor weakness following trauma to the limb. CRPS is a clinical diagnosis. The absence of fever or other signs of infection makes cellulitis unlikely. The examination findings make factitious disorder and functional neurologic symptom disorder (conversion disorder) unlikely. The absence of the characteristic stocking-glove altered sensation makes peripheral neuropathy unlikely.

QuestionStem: A 28-year-old nulligravida has a 2- to 3-day history of severe lower abdominal pain. On examination the patientâ€™s temperature is 37.7 Â°C (99.9 Â°F), bowel sounds are normal, and there is diffuse lower abdominal tenderness without rebound. On pelvic examination there is adnexal tenderness and pain on cervical motion. Vaginal discharge shows leukocytes. A pregnancy test is negative. In addition to sexually transmitted infection testing and empiric treatment, which one of the following should you recommend? A) No further testing B) A repeat pregnancy test in 7 days C) Pelvic ultrasonography D) Pelvic MRI E) Laparoscopy
Explanation: This patient's likely diagnosis is pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), and the diagnosis can be made clinically in most cases. In women at risk of PID, treatment should be initiated if any of the following are present: cervical motion tenderness, uterine tenderness, or adnexal tenderness. These criteria are considered sensitive for the diagnosis, but the clinician should be mindful to evaluate for other causes, such as appendicitis or tubo- ovarian abscess (TOA) as indicated by the history and physical examination. Appendicitis would likely cause unilateral pain. TOA often presents acutely with unilateral pain on examination and an adnexal mass. These findings may prompt the clinician to pursue additional testing such as pelvic ultrasonography. A pelvic ultrasound may show fluid-filled or thickened tubes in the setting of PID, but it is not routinely indicated. MRI may be necessary if an ultrasound were to demonstrate TOA or findings other than simple PID. CT of the abdomen may be completed to evaluate complications above the pelvis. Laparoscopy would not be the initial evaluation. This patient is acutely ill and repeating a pregnancy test a week later would not be appropriate.

QuestionStem: A 65-year-old male with a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to the urgent care clinic approximately 20 hours after experiencing an episode of sudden-onset right-hand weakness. He was suddenly unable to grasp his utensils while eating dinner. These symptoms resolved after about 20 minutes. His wife had encouraged him to seek urgent evaluation, but he declined because his symptoms had resolved. Today the patient's vital signs include a blood pressure of 124/72 mm Hg, a heart rate of 80 beats/min, and a respiratory rate of 12/min. The neurologic examination findings are normal. An EKG shows normal sinus rhythm. The ABCD2 score is 4. Further management includes which one of the following regimens? A) Aspirin, 81 mg daily B) Clopidogrel (Plavix), 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily C) Clopidogrel, 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily on days 2â€“10; plus aspirin, 81 mg daily D) Clopidogrel, 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily on days 2â€“21; plus aspirin, 81 mg daily
Explanation: In patients presenting with a moderate- to high-risk TIA or minor stroke within 24 hours of symptom onset, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) for 21 days prior to transitioning to a single antiplatelet agent decreases the 90-day stroke risk from 7.8% to 5.2% compared to aspirin alone. This patient has symptoms consistent with a high-risk TIA based on an ABCD2 score (age, blood pressure, clinical features of TIA, duration, and diabetes mellitus history) of 4. Recommended management includes loading doses of clopidogrel (300â€“600 mg) and aspirin (50â€“325 mg) within 24 hours of symptom onset followed by clopidogrel (75 mg) and aspirin (50â€“325 mg daily) for 21 days. The patient can then transition to a single antiplatelet agent. Initial treatment with a single antiplatelet agent or with DAPT for less than 21 days does not reduce stroke risk compared to DAPT for 21 days.

QuestionStem: A 55-year-old patient with a previous history of opioid use disorder and untreated hypertension presents to your office to establish care. They are now in recovery and would like to catch up on screening laboratory studies. Their blood pressure today is 160/90 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show elevated AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase levels and are positive for hepatitis C antibody and hepatitis C viral load. Testing for hepatitis B and HIV is negative. Lipids are elevated. You calculate an atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease 10-year risk score of 10%. A right upper quadrant ultrasound shows fibrotic changes of the liver. The patient is surprised that they have liver damage, as they feel well and have never consumed much alcohol. In addition to referring the patient to a gastroenterologist, which one of the following should you begin now to improve fibrotic changes? A) Treatment of hepatitis C B) Prophylactic antibiotics C) A nonselective Î²-blocker D) Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV E) Upper endoscopy with banding of any varices
Reference: Smith A, Baumgartner K, Bositis C. Cirrhosis: diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2019;100(12):759-770.
Explanation: Treatment of hepatitis C in patients with metabolic dysfunctionâ€“associated steatohepatitis (formerly known as nonalcoholic steatohepatitis) can improve fibrotic changes, as can bariatric surgery. Prophylactic antibiotics should be initiated to prevent spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in patients with a history of SBP; low ascitic fluid protein levels (<1.5 g/dL); advanced hepatic disease as indicated by a Child-Pugh score â‰¥9 or a bilirubin level â‰¥3 mg/dL; or renal disease as indicated by a serum creatinine level â‰¥1.2 mg/dL or a serum sodium level â‰¤130 mEq/L. Nonselective Î²-blockers should be started in patients with significant varices seen on upper endoscopy. Coinfection with hepatitis C virus and HIV increases a patient's risk of fibrotic progression to cirrhosis. A conversation regarding risks and benefits of initiating pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) would be beneficial; however, PrEP has not been shown to improve fibrotic changes. Offering treatment for viral hepatitis along with PrEP increases access for patients who are at risk of coinfection. In individuals with metabolic dysfunctionâ€“associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) (formerly known as nonalcoholic fatty liver disease) and cirrhosis, antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended in specific scenarios: in patients with a history of SBP, during episodes of acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding, and in select high-risk patients with low ascitic fluid protein levels (<1.5 g/dL) along with either advanced liver disease (Child-Pugh score â‰¥9 and a bilirubin level â‰¥3 mg/dL) or signs of renal impairment (a creatinine level â‰¥1.2 mg/dL, a BUN level â‰¥25 mg/dL, or a sodium level â‰¤130 mEq/L).

QuestionStem: A 46-year-old female sees you for an annual well woman visit. Her only concern is pelvic pressure that becomes more bothersome throughout the day. She notes occasional urinary incontinence of small amounts when coughing or running. She says these symptoms mildly bother her, and mostly wants to make sure there is not a more serious problem. On physical examination, using the lower half of a bivalve speculum, you note a prolapse of the anterior wall, 0.5 cm proximal to the hymenal ring. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step for this patient? A) Reassurance only B) Pelvic floor muscle training C) Topical estrogen D) Surgical consultation
Explanation: Pelvic organ prolapse is a common problem, and can be described as apical, of the anterior wall, or of the posterior wall. The Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification system can be used to evaluate the prolapse and determine grading. A grade 2 prolapse indicates a prolapse that extends within 1 cm above or below the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscle training can reduce prolapse by 1â€“2 cm and provide subjective improvement in symptoms. There is no clear evidence of the benefit of estrogen alone for pelvic organ prolapse. Surgical management is often the ultimate treatment for pelvic organ prolapse, but it is important to note that urinary incontinence can worsen after repair. This patient is minimally bothered by her symptoms; therefore, surgical treatment would not be recommended at this time but may be indicated in the future. Pelvic organ prolapse is generally progressive until menopause, after which it can alternate between regression and progression.

QuestionStem: A 72-year-old male with a past medical history of coronary artery disease, COPD, and hyperlipidemia sees you because of an acute onset of left lower leg pain 1 hour ago. He was in his usual state of health when the pain occurred. His left foot is numb and cold, and he is having difficulty walking. He has a 60-pack-year smoking history. Physical examination findings include a mottled appearance of the left lower leg and foot. Posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses are absent. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step in management? A) Rivaroxaban (Xarelto) B) Stat duplex ultrasonography C) Stat CT angiography D) Expedited outpatient referral to a vascular surgeon E) Transfer to the nearest emergency department with access to a vascular surgeon
Explanation: This patient's presentation is concerning for acute limb ischemia and he requires immediate transfer to an emergency facility with vascular surgeon accessibility (SOR C). Anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin should be started soon after arrival. Acute limb ischemia is a vascular emergency as skeletal muscle can only survive 4â€“6 hours without blood flow. Acute limb ischemia is characterized by the 6 Pâ€™s: pain, pallor, poikilothermia, pulselessness, paresthesia, and paralysis. Direct-acting oral anticoagulants can be used to manage symptomatic peripheral artery disease (PAD), but not for acute management of limb ischemia. While duplex ultrasonography or CT angiography can be used to determine the presence and location of the obstructed vessel, transfer to an emergency facility should not be delayed for imaging. Referral to a vascular surgeon would delay treatment and would be more appropriate for chronic PAD symptoms.

QuestionStem: A 22-year-old male presents with a 5-day history of left lower leg pain over the midshaft of his tibia. Two months ago, he started training for a half marathon. Radiographs performed yesterday at an urgent care clinic were normal. You suspect a bone stress injury. Which one of the following is the preferred definitive imaging modality to evaluate for a bone stress injury? A) Repeat radiography in 1 week B) Point-of-care ultrasonography C) CT D) MRI E) Nuclear bone scintigraphy
Explanation: Bone stress injuries are typically overuse injuries that are caused by an accumulation of bone microdamage. They often occur after an increase in the intensity, frequency, or interval of training. They can also occur when there is structurally compromised bone (osteoporosis, tumors, infections) as normal physiologic loads can damage such compromised bone. Radiography is the initial imaging modality because of its high specificity, despite its low sensitivity. If the radiograph is negative and the patient remains symptomatic, then MRI is the preferred follow-up study for definitive diagnosis. It is preferred over CT and nuclear bone scintigraphy because it has higher sensitivity and specificity and avoids the use of radiation. Repeat radiography is not the preferred definitive imaging modality because radiographs are often negative when taken within the first 2â€“3 weeks of symptoms. Point-of-care ultrasonography can be used by trained clinicians as an adjunct modality, but its sensitivity is not as high as the sensitivity of MRI.

QuestionStem: A 32-year-old female presents to an urgent care clinic with a flare-up of her usually well- controlled chronic urticaria. She takes cetirizine (Zyrtec), 10 mg daily. Which one of the following should you recommend? A) A pseudoallergen-free diet B) Doubling the cetirizine dosage C) A nasal corticosteroid D) Cyclosporine (Sandimmune) E) Omalizumab (Xolair)
Reference: Chow AW, Benninger MS, Brook I, et al: IDSA clinical practice guideline for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis in children and adults. Clin Infect Dis 2012;54(8):e72-e112. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021.
Explanation: The first-line treatment of chronic urticaria is an oral second-generation H -antihistamine. 1 If that is not effective, a reasonable next step is to increase the H -antihistamine, up to 4 1 times the usual FDA-recommended dosing. Current evidence does not strongly support the effectiveness of a pseudoallergen-free diet in managing chronic urticaria. Nasal corticosteroids are effective for allergic rhinitis but have not been found to be useful for chronic urticaria. A short course of oral corticosteroids, such as prednisone, can be effective as a short-term solution for a flare-up but is not recommended for chronic control. Cyclosporine is only recommended for patients with severe refractory symptoms that have not responded to increased dosages of second-generation antihistamines and a trial of H -blockers. Omalizumab is effective for chronic urticaria and would be indicated if 2 aggressive antihistamine therapy is unsuccessful.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old female comes to your office with an 8-day history of nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and cough. Based on guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America, this patient should be treated with antibiotic therapy if she has which one of the following additional symptoms? A) A fever up to 38.0 Â°C (100.4 Â°F) B) Halitosis and tooth pain C) An increase in nasal discharge after an initial improvement of symptoms D) Purulent rhinorrhea
Explanation: It is difficult to adequately distinguish between viral sinusitis and acute bacterial rhinosinusitis. However, most instances of acute bacterial rhinosinusitis improve without antibiotics. Major symptoms of sinusitis include purulent nasal discharge, nasal congestion, facial pain or pressure, decreased smell, and fever. Minor symptoms include headaches, cough, fatigue, ear pain or pressure, halitosis, and tooth pain. If adequate follow-up can be ensured, watchful waiting and symptomatic management is recommended for up to 10 days. Patients with severe symptoms (fever â‰¥39 Â°C with purulent nasal discharge and/or facial pain) or those with double sickening (new symptoms following improvement of an upper respiratory infection) should be treated immediately with empiric antibiotics.

QuestionStem: You are seeing a 44-year-old female who has a 25-pack-year smoking history. She quit smoking 6 years ago after a friend received a diagnosis of lung cancer. According to U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommendations, lung cancer screening should be initiated at what age in this patient? A) No screening is indicated B) 45 C) 50 D) 55 E) 60
Explanation: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death in the United States, with approximately 90% of cases linked to smoking. In 2021, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommended annual screening with low-dose CT in adults 50â€“80 years of age who have a 20-pack-year smoking history and either currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years.

QuestionStem: A 25-year-old male presents with a 5-day history of dysuria and mucopurulent penile discharge. On examination the urethral meatus is erythematous and a small amount of discharge is noted. A urine dipstick is positive for leukocyte esterase and a nucleic acid amplification test is negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment? A) Azithromycin (Zithromax), 1 g orally once B) Levofloxacin, 500 mg orally for 7 days C) Ceftriaxone, 500 mg intramuscularly once D) Gentamicin, 240 mg intramuscularly once, plus azithromycin, 2 g orally once E) Metronidazole, 2 g orally once, plus doxycycline, 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
Reference: Sell J, Nasir M, Courchesne C: Urethritis: Rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician 2021;103(9):553-558.
Explanation: This patient has nongonococcal urethritis, which should be treated with azithromycin. Symptoms of urethritis include dysuria, purulent or mucopurulent urethral discharge, and urethral discomfort and/or erythema. Diagnostic criteria include typical signs and symptoms or a history of exposure along with â‰¥10 WBCs/hpf on a first-void urinalysis, â‰¥2 WBCs/oil immersion field found on Gram stain of the discharge, or a positive leukocyte esterase with first-void urine. At least half of nongonococcal urethritis cases have no known etiologic agent. Of the cases with known etiologies, Mycoplasma genitalium is responsible for 15%â€“20%. Treatment options for M genitalium include azithromycin, 1 g orally; doxycycline, 100 mg twice daily for 1 week; or moxifloxacin, 400 mg orally for 7â€“14 days. If the results of nucleic acid amplification testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia are unavailable, then intramuscular ceftriaxone plus a 7-day course of oral doxycycline would be appropriate and can be used for empiric therapy while awaiting results. Intramuscular gentamicin plus oral azithromycin is used as an alternative treatment regimen for Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Metronidazole is used to treat Trichomonas, which represents 1%â€“ 8% of cases in men and should be diagnosed using a molecular-based test. Levofloxacin is a treatment option for chlamydia and is not first line.

QuestionStem: A 55-year-old female presents to the emergency department with what she describes as the worst headache of her life that began suddenly 1 hour ago. Her medical history is significant for tobacco use and uncontrolled hypertension. She indicates that she has had a few headaches over the last month, but today's headache came on suddenly and is extremely intense, unlike the previous headaches. There is no history of head trauma. Her accompanying relative adds that the patient has been experiencing waxing and waning levels of consciousness since the sudden onset of the headache. She has vomited several times, secondary to pain from the headache. Which one of the following is the most important, immediate next step in management? A) Morphine B) Ondansetron C) Head CT D) Brain MRI E) Lumbar puncture
Reference: Winslow BT, Vandal C, Dang L. Fibromyalgia: diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(2):137-144. | Baird D, Hoffman A: Use of amphetamines for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder in adults. Am Fam Physician 2019;100(5):278-279.
Explanation: This patient's clinical picture is most consistent with a subarachnoid hemorrhage. Patients typically describe this as a sudden onset of the worst headache they have experienced. The most immediate and meaningful next step is to order a CT scan of the head to verify the diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage. The diagnosis and management of subarachnoid hemorrhage is time sensitive. Neurosurgery should be consulted immediately if subarachnoid hemorrhage is verified. Brain MRI would likely take longer to obtain than head CT and is not diagnostically advantageous in subarachnoid hemorrhage compared to a head CT. Pain and nausea control are reasonable, but a CT scan of the head should take precedence because of the time sensitivity of diagnosis and management. A lumbar puncture should be considered if the head CT scan is negative but clinical suspicion for subarachnoid hemorrhage remains high, especially if the presentation occurs >6 hours after onset.

QuestionStem: A 28-year-old male who uses cannabis daily presents with a 2-week history of persistent nausea and vomiting. He notes little relief from previously prescribed ondansetron but does feel better during a hot shower. While tapering his cannabis use, which one of the following would be most likely to improve this patient's nausea? A) Topical diclofenac gel B) Haloperidol C) Metoclopramide (Reglan) D) Prednisone E) Prochlorperazine
Explanation: This patient has cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome, characterized by heavy chronic cannabis use and nausea that improves with hot baths or showers. Current evidence suggests that haloperidol is more effective than more traditionally used antiemetics, such as metoclopramide or prochlorperazine, or corticosteroids in managing nausea associated with this syndrome. Topical capsaicin has also been shown to be effective. Topical capsaicin, when applied to the upper abdomen, may help alleviate symptoms by activating transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) receptors. This mechanism can lead to desensitization of sensory nerve fibers, potentially reducing the need for antiemetic medications. Benzodiazepines may help acutely, and tricyclic antidepressants may have efficacy for longer-term management. Cessation of cannabis use is necessary for definitive treatment. Diclofenac gel is not appropriate pharmacotherapy for cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome.

QuestionStem: A 30-year-old female with fibromyalgia sees you for follow-up. Her symptoms include widespread pain and prominent fatigue. At your advice, she has engaged in a trial of multidisciplinary, nonpharmacologic treatment including structured exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and therapeutic massage, and has experienced only partial improvement in her symptoms. She expresses interest in trying a medication to treat her pain. Which one of the following medications would be most likely to improve this patient's pain? A) Acetaminophen B) Duloxetine (Cymbalta) C) Gabapentin (Neurontin) D) Hydrocodone/acetaminophen E) Ibuprofen
Explanation: Duloxetine is an SNRI that is effective for treating fibromyalgia pain (SOR A). Other medications with level A evidence of effectiveness in reducing fibromyalgia-related pain include amitriptyline, cyclobenzaprine, and pregabalin. The SNRI milnacipran also has level A evidence for reducing fibromyalgia-related pain and has evidence for reducing fatigue and improving cognition in patients with fibromyalgia. Other SNRIs, including venlafaxine, have not been studied as extensively. Current evidence is insufficient to support acetaminophen or gabapentin use to treat fibromyalgia pain. Similarly, there is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of opioids such as hydrocodone, which are associated with a significant risk of adverse effects, dependence, misuse, and worsening hyperalgesia. NSAIDs such as ibuprofen are no better than placebo and introduce significant risk of adverse effects.

QuestionStem: An 8-year-old male is brought to your office by his parents because of concerns about ongoing nocturnal enuresis. He wets his bed several nights per week despite behavioral interventions. He has no daytime urinary symptoms. Physical examination findings and urinalysis are normal. You discuss treatment options and the parents indicate a preference for medication, as the patient will be attending an overnight summer camp in several weeks. Which one of the following would be the preferred pharmacologic treatment for this childâ€™s nocturnal enuresis? A) Desmopressin (DDAVP) B) Imipramine C) Oxybutynin D) Tamsulosin (Flomax)
Explanation: Behavioral methods and enuresis alarms are first-line treatments for monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis without an identifiable cause. If pharmacotherapy is initiated, intranasal or oral desmopressin is acceptable. Imipramine and oxybutynin are additional options if there is no response to oral desmopressin. Tamsulosin is not recommended for this indication.

QuestionStem: A 25-year-old male with a childhood diagnosis of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presents for re-evaluation of ADHD. He did not take medication for ADHD as a child. He is now having problems with attention and concentration that are affecting his work. He was recently placed on a stimulant medication, but had to discontinue it when his blood pressure and heart rate became elevated. He is still interested in pharmacologic therapy. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next medication? A) Atomoxetine (Strattera) B) Clonidine C) Escitalopram (Lexapro) D) Guanfacine E) Nortriptyline (Pamelor)
Explanation: Stimulants are first-line pharmacotherapy for adults with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) given the extensive data supporting their effectiveness. However, there are potential adverse effects that can prevent patients from taking them, such as headaches, insomnia, poor appetite, weight loss, anxiety, depression, elevated heart rate, and elevated blood pressure. When stimulants are not tolerated or do not provide an adequate response, the second-line therapy would be an SNRI such as atomoxetine or viloxazine. Clonidine and guanfacine are only approved for the treatment of ADHD in children and adolescents. Although they are sometimes used off-label in adults, there is limited evidence to support such use. Tricyclic antidepressants such as nortriptyline have limited evidence and are not approved for ADHD treatment. Escitalopram is not effective for treating ADHD.

QuestionStem: A 19-year-old college basketball player consults you for follow-up after sustaining an anterior shoulder dislocation for the first time. He initially presented to the emergency department where his shoulder was reduced and immobilized in a sling. Which one of the following is most likely to decrease the risk of future recurrence? A) Continued sling use for 4 weeks B) Completion of a course of physical therapy C) Administration of an intra-articular corticosteroid injection D) Surgical repair
Explanation: Although anterior shoulder dislocations have a high rate of recurrence (up to 90%), evidence from cohort studies has consistently found a lower rate of recurrence in active patients <25 years of age who have received surgical treatment for first-time dislocations. Because of this patient's age and activity level, referral for surgical repair would be appropriate. Other treatment options for anterior shoulder dislocation include the use of a sling for 2â€“4 weeks with graduated rehabilitation of passive and active range of motion. If no surgery is warranted, athletes can return to play when they are pain free with symmetrical shoulder range of motion and able to perform sport-specific motions, which can be as early as 2â€“3 weeks after injury. Intra-articular corticosteroids are not advised after a shoulder dislocation.

QuestionStem: A 32-year-old gravida 2 para 2 female who is 3 weeks post partum presents to the local urgent care center for left breast pain, swelling, and redness that started a few hours ago. She does not have fever or chills. A breast examination is significant for warm, tender, and mildly erythematous nodules localized to the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. In addition to initiating NSAIDs, which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management? A) Applying cold compresses to the affected breast B) Applying warm compresses to the affected breast C) Using a manual breast pump on the affected breast for complete breast emptying D) Obtaining a CBC and C-reactive protein levels E) Obtaining a breast milk culture
Explanation: Mastitis is a spectrum of inflammatory breast conditions, which includes lactational or inflammatory mastitis, bacterial mastitis, breast abscess, postpartum breast engorgement, and more. Patients with inflammatory mastitis exhibit unilateral breast erythema, swelling, and pain and may experience systemic inflammatory responses such as fever, malaise, or headache. The initial steps in managing inflammatory mastitis are supportive care with NSAIDs, cold compresses, and watchful waiting. Inflammatory mastitis is diagnosed clinically and does not require laboratory testing or imaging. If a patient's symptoms worsen or do not improve within 24â€“48 hours with conservative measures, narrow-spectrum antibiotics targeting the most common organisms (Staphylococcus species and Streptococcus species) should be empirically initiated for bacterial mastitis. Warm compresses and frequent breast pumping for complete breast emptying to avoid milk stasis are no longer recommended. CBC and C-reactive protein testing are not helpful as these levels can be elevated in both inflammatory and bacterial mastitis. Breast milk cultures are seldom necessary because they may produce false- positive results caused by normal skin bacteria colonization.

QuestionStem: A young couple in your practice presents for preconception counseling. Both partners are known carriers of cystic fibrosis (CF). They ask what risk this poses to their future children. Which one of the following accurately reflects the risk of CF disease transmission to any potential offspring of this couple? A) 25% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier B) 25% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier C) 50% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier D) 50% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier
Explanation: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder with an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance. People who are affected by the disease are homozygous for the condition, with 2 copies of the mutated gene. Carriers are heterozygous for the condition with 1 mutated gene and 1 normal gene. When both partners in a couple are carriers, any potential offspring has a 25% chance of being affected, a 50% chance of being an unaffected carrier, and a 25% chance of being an unaffected noncarrier.

QuestionStem: A 47-year-old nurse presents with a 9-month history of fatigue and brain fog that has kept them from working. Their symptoms began after a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19. They report sleeping at least 7 hours per night but wake unrefreshed and experience lightheadedness throughout the day. They used to run 4 miles 5 days per week but now report feeling exhausted and experience energy crashes after showering and getting dressed. The patient's vital signs and BMI are normal. A physical examination is unremarkable. Their Patient Health Questionnaireâ€“9 (PHQ-9) score is 3. A CBC, TSH level, basic metabolic panel, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are within the normal range. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Depression B) Fibromyalgia C) Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) D) Obstructive sleep apnea E) Systemic lupus erythematosus
Explanation: Based on the National Academy of Medicine criteria for myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), this patient has demonstrated the core symptoms of ME/CFS (unrefreshing sleep, new-onset fatigue, and postexertional malaise) for at least 6 months, in addition to brain fog and lightheadedness. Notably, their symptoms developed after a previous COVID-19 infection, an illness that is commonly associated with ME/CFS. A Patient Health Questionnaireâ€“9 (PHQ-9) score of 3 indicates no or minimal depression, which would not account for severe fatigue or energy crashes after minimal exertion. Exercise may actually improve depression. Secondary causes of fatigue, such as fibromyalgia and systemic lupus erythematosus, typically improve with exercise. Although obstructive sleep apnea may cause unrefreshing sleep, it is not associated with profound fatigue and postexertional malaise.

QuestionStem: A 62-year-old male is brought to the clinic by his wife for fatigue. His wife says he has been sleeping in the recliner rather than in bed and has been less active over the last several weeks. He reports that his clothes have been fitting tightly and he has gained weight despite feeling full early in a meal. He also notes difficulty bending over to tie his shoes and pet the dog. He has no chest pain or pressure and has not traveled recently. His past medical history is notable for a myocardial infarction 5 years ago treated with a stent, and elevated cholesterol. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit 20 years ago. His medications include aspirin, 81 mg daily; lisinopril (Zestril), 20 mg daily; and metoprolol succinate (Toprol-XL), 50 mg daily. On examination he has a blood pressure of 110/65 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 89 beats/min, and an oxygen saturation of 95% on room air. You also note mildly increased work of breathing, a regular heart rate, and a laterally displaced apical impulse. Which one of the following tests would most likely confirm this patientâ€™s underlying diagnosis? A) EKG B) Echocardiography C) X-ray of the chest D) CT angiography of the heart E) CT angiography of the lungs
Reference: Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline for the management of heart failure: a report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association joint committee on clinical practice guidelines. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032.
Explanation: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is a major public health concern affecting more than 6.5 million U.S. adults. Family physicians should be familiar with its typical clinical presentations and diagnosis, which is best established using echocardiography. Symptoms vary and may include dyspnea, ankle edema, and fatigue. Orthopnea, as evident by modification of sleeping from supine to reclined or semi- reclined, is also common. Symptoms of right-sided heart failure, such as early satiety, abdominal bloating, ill-fitting clothes, and shortness of breath when bent forward, are also frequently encountered, but are less specific. Physical examination may reveal signs of congestion such as elevated jugular venous pressure, S gallop, or a laterally displaced 3 apical impulse. Some predictors of HFrEF include increased heart rate, coronary artery disease (hazard ratio [HR] 1.73), and previous myocardial infarction (MI) (HR 3.49). The diagnosis is made by documenting left ventricular systolic dysfunction with a left ventricular ejection fraction â‰¤40% and an elevated BNP level. An EKG is a key component of the workup and evaluation of contributing factors, such as arrhythmia or acute MI, but not diagnosis. A chest x-ray, while quick and readily available, may identify cardiomegaly or vascular congestion but is normal in approximately 20% of those with acute HFrEF, limiting diagnostic utility. Given this patient's history of MI, CT angiography would be inappropriate and is typically reserved for evaluating coronary artery disease in low-risk patients. CT angiography is used to evaluate for pulmonary embolism, which is less likely, given this patient's lack of immobility, prolonged travel, or other risk factors, as well as a history and physical examination that favor heart failure (displaced apical impulse, orthopnea, abdominal distention/weight gain).

QuestionStem: A 3-year-old male is brought to your office by his parents for a routine well child visit. He is up to date on vaccines and the parents have no concerns to discuss today. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, this child should be screened for which one of the following conditions? A) Amblyopia B) Anemia C) Autism spectrum disorder D) Dental caries E) Hearing loss
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016. | Final Recommendation Statement: Vitamin D Deficiency: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2014. | US Preventive Services Task Force. Final recommendation statement: prevention of dental caries in children younger than 5 years: screening and interventions. Updated December 7, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016.
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends vision screening at least once in all children 3â€“5 years of age to detect amblyopia or its risk factors (B recommendation). Screening may be performed using visual acuity testing with picture identification, Snellen or "tumbling E," or by an automated device that assesses visual acuity. Early identification of vision disorders can help prevent permanent vision loss due to amblyopia. Anemia screening is recommended for all children at 1 year of age by the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP), but this approach was found to have insufficient evidence of benefit by the USPSTF (I recommendation). Screening for autism spectrum disorder is recommended by the AAP at 18 and 24 months of age, but the USPSTF found insufficient evidence that universal screening results in improved care in families without concerns about their child's development (I recommendation). The USPSTF found insufficient evidence of benefit of screening for dental caries in children <5 years of age (I recommendation). Instead, it recommends that fluoride varnish be applied every 3â€“6 months in all children 6 months to 5 years of age to prevent dental caries (B recommendation). This should be performed in the family physician's office if it has not been applied by a dentist. Additionally, an oral fluoride supplement is recommended to prevent dental caries in children without adequate fluoride in their water supply (B recommendation). Universal hearing screening is recommended for newborns, but screening at this age is not recommended.

QuestionStem: A 50-year-old female comes to your clinic for an annual wellness examination. Her past medical history is notable for dermatomyositis diagnosed 2 years ago. She is otherwise healthy and her BMI is normal. Patients with dermatomyositis are at increased risk of which one of the following conditions? A) Chronic kidney disease B) Diabetes mellitus C) Hyperlipidemia D) Malignancy E) Obesity
Reference: Mounsey A, Barzin A, Rietz A: Functional dyspepsia: Evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician 2020;101(2):84-88. | Sell J, Ramirez S, Partin M. Parathyroid disorders. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(3):289-298.
Explanation: While counseling on diet and exercise and screening for diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia are important, malignancy screening is the most important component of the wellness visit in this patient with dermatomyositis diagnosed after the age of 40. Cancer is a leading cause of death in this patient population and is diagnosed in up to 25% of these patients within 3 years of their dermatomyositis diagnosis. The most likely cancers in this population include adenocarcinomas of the ovary, lung, pancreas, stomach, and colon as well as non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Population-level cancer screening recommendations are generally insufficient for this population, and advanced cancer screening modalities, including CT scans and PET scans, may be recommended based on the patient's individual risk factors to target these types of cancers. Chronic kidney disease and obesity have not been observed to have an increased risk in patients with dermatomyositis.

QuestionStem: Of the following cognitive biases, which one best describes the practice of prioritizing data that supports an initial impression, even when the initial impression is incorrect? A) Anchoring B) Availability C) Implicit D) Outcomes
Explanation: Anchoring bias describes the practice of physicians prioritizing information and data that supports their initial impressions, even when these impressions are incorrect. In other words, it is when physicians are biased toward their first impression. Availability bias describes the likelihood of a diagnosis being erroneously linked to how easy it is to think of examples. Implicit bias is defined as attitudes and beliefs about race, ethnicity, age, ability, gender, or other characteristics. Implicit bias operates on an unconscious level and can only be measured indirectly. Outcomes bias refers to the attribution of all results to prior decisions, regardless of whether there is a reason to do so.

QuestionStem: An otherwise healthy 15-year-old female presents for evaluation of a 7-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Her periods are 10 days long, irregular, and require 10 tampons per day. She sometimes passes large clots. Other than some fatigue, she feels well today and her vital signs are normal. A point-of-care hemoglobin level is 10.2 g/dL (N 12.0â€“16.0). Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation? A) Testing for a bleeding disorder B) FSH and LH levels C) Estrogen and progesterone levels D) Pelvic ultrasonography E) Pelvic MRI
Explanation: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) is very common in adolescents and may be a sign of an underlying bleeding disorder. Although bleeding disorders affect 1%â€“2% of the general population, the prevalence increases to 20% among adolescent girls with heavy menstrual bleeding. Platelet dysfunction and von Willebrand disease are most commonly identified. Evaluation for bleeding disorders is indicated when there is frequent pad or tampon use (>1 every 1â€“2 hours); menstrual bleeding lasting longer than 7 days; passage of clots larger than 1 inch in diameter; a personal history of anemia treatment, excessive bleeding with tooth extraction or surgery, or excessive bruising; or a family history of a bleeding disorder. The initial workup for HMB in an adolescent includes a CBC; a prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and INR; a von Willebrand panel; and hCG, ferritin, fibrinogen, and TSH levels. Testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea should be considered in sexually active patients. Testosterone, prolactin, and DHEA levels should be obtained if there are signs of polycystic ovary syndrome or other endocrine disorders. Levels of FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone are not helpful in the evaluation of excessive uterine bleeding. Pelvic imaging is not routinely necessary but should be considered for HMB that does not respond to initial management. Hormonal contraceptives and tranexamic acid may be used for the treatment of HMB, but workup for a bleeding disorder is the most important first step.

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old patient presents with a 6-month history of epigastric burning pain that occurs several days per week. The pain does not seem to have a clear correlation to eating. There has been no substernal pain or regurgitation and no weight loss, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, fever, night sweats, or fatigue. The patient has no prior medical history and takes no medications, but has tried calcium carbonate occasionally without benefit. A physical examination reveals normal vital signs and no abdominal abnormalities or enlarged lymph nodes. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Sucralfate (Carafate) B) Stool antigen testing for Helicobacter pylori C) Serum testing for H pylori D) Abdominal ultrasonography E) Referral for upper endoscopy
Explanation: This patient presents with symptoms of dyspepsia. Dyspepsia is a diagnosis of exclusion and should be considered if at least 1 of the following symptoms is present: early satiety, epigastric pain, epigastric burning, and postprandial fullness. The majority of patients diagnosed with dyspepsia have functional dyspepsia with no evidence of organic disease on endoscopy. Testing for and treating Helicobacter pylori infection is recommended in patients with dyspepsia, with evidence of improvement 1 year after treatment. The stool antigen test and the urea breath test are the preferred noninvasive options for detecting H pylori. Serum testing for H pylori is not recommended because it has lower sensitivity and specificity than other noninvasive tests. Sucralfate has not been shown to be effective for dyspepsia. Abdominal ultrasonography, particularly of the upper abdomen, is indicated when evaluating for liver pathology or gallbladder disease but is not useful for investigating dyspepsia. Endoscopy is recommended for patients >60 years of age with new dyspepsia, and for patients <60 years of age with multiple or severe alarm symptoms, such as marked weight loss, rapidly progressive dysphagia, an epigastric mass, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

QuestionStem: A 48-year-old female comes to your office for a health maintenance examination. She is currently asymptomatic but has a medical history of renal calculi. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 12.1 mg/dL (N 8.7â€“10.2), confirmed by an ionized calcium level. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step at this time? A) A 24-hour urinary calcium level B) A parathyroid hormone level C) A vitamin D level D) CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Explanation: Hypercalcemia is commonly identified through a metabolic panel and should be confirmed with a corrected or ionized calcium level. The most common cause of hypercalcemia is primary hyperparathyroidism, but any underlying malignancy should be ruled out. A thorough history should assess for symptoms such as arrhythmias, hypertension, abdominal pain, arthralgias, fractures, mental status changes, anxiety, depression, and nephrolithiasis. Dietary intake of calcium and phosphorus, use of medications such as lithium, and a family history of multiple endocrine neoplasia are also important to evaluate. After confirming hypercalcemia, the initial test should be a parathyroid hormone (PTH) level. If the PTH level is elevated, then 25-hydroxyvitamin D, creatinine, and 24-hour urinary calcium levels should be measured in addition to ordering a bone density test. Imaging of the parathyroid glands is not necessary for diagnosis, but is useful if surgical management is indicated. A low PTH level could suggest malignancy and may indicate the need for a CT scan.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old female presents to your office with a 3-day history of right-sided facial weakness. She has flattening of the nasolabial fold and is unable to elevate the right corner of her mouth. She is able to close her right eye and raise her eyebrows. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management? A) Reassurance only B) Prednisone plus valacyclovir (Valtrex) C) MRI of the brain D) Plasmapheresis E) Surgical decompression
Explanation: This patient presents with a relatively acute onset of facial weakness affecting only the right side of the face, but she is able to close her right eye and raise her eyebrows. Sparing of the forehead muscles is consistent with a supranuclear lesion because the nucleus of cranial nerve VII receives fibers from both sides of the cortex for the upper face, meaning the forehead would be unaffected. Cranial nerve VII includes visceral efferent fibers (facial muscles and stapedius), visceral motor fibers (salivary and lacrimal glands), and special sensory fibers (taste to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue). In Bell palsy, which involves edema of this cranial nerve, the entire side of the face is affected, including the forehead. Therefore, this presentation is not consistent with Bell palsy and warrants further evaluation, including brain MRI, to assess for a central cause. Prednisone and valacyclovir are not appropriate for this patient, as they are treatments used for Bell palsy. Plasmapheresis is not indicated for Bell palsy, stroke, or tumor. Surgical decompression is not indicated.

QuestionStem: A previously healthy 70-year-old yoga instructor is brought to the emergency department (ED) by her students after falling while practicing yoga. Her students report that she struck her head on the floor and was unresponsive for a few seconds, but recovered quickly. She was slightly confused after the injury, but was able to stand and describe the incident. On initial assessment in the ED, she is holding her head in obvious discomfort and a hematoma is developing on her forehead. She is alert and oriented, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of
Explanation: In an older adult, head trauma with a loss of consciousness, vomiting, anterograde amnesia, or a previous history of head trauma requires CT of the head. Being older than 65 years is considered an independent risk factor for head trauma. Older adults with a traumatic brain injury experience higher morbidity and mortality due to age-related changes in vasculature that leave vessels more vulnerable to rupture and changes in white matter that render white matter tracts more susceptible to shear injury. As a result of these physiological changes, older patients are more susceptible to injury, and even those with minor mechanisms of injury and no abnormalities on a neurologic examination may still have subdural or epidural bleeding. NSAIDs carry a risk of bleeding and should be avoided following a traumatic brain injury.

QuestionStem: A 3-day-old infant presents to your clinic for a routine check-up. The parents report that the infant has been feeding well but has had fewer wet diapers than expected. On examination, the infant appears slightly lethargic with dry skin. You need to assess the hydration status of the infant. Which one of the following would be the most reliable clinical sign for assessing dehydration in this 3-day-old infant? A) Capillary refill time B) Decreased urine output C) Dry mucous membranes D) Skin turgor E) Sunken fontanelle
Explanation: Capillary refill time is the most reliable clinical sign for assessing dehydration in infants. An abnormal capillary refill time (>2 seconds) is a strong indicator of dehydration and has been shown to have a high likelihood ratio for predicting dehydration in children. While decreased urine output is a sensitive indicator of dehydration, it is not specific and can be influenced by other factors such as feeding patterns and ambient temperature. Dry mucous membranes are a useful sign but can be subjective and less reliable compared to capillary refill time. Abnormal skin turgor is more commonly used in older children and is less reliable in neonates due to their higher skin elasticity. A sunken fontanelle can indicate dehydration, but it is less specific and can be influenced by other factors, such as the patient's position during the examination.

QuestionStem: A 16-year-old female presents with her parents because she has been experiencing a loss of interest in her usual activities, trouble sleeping, difficulty concentrating, and low energy over the last month. After a discussion with the patient and her parents, you refer her for cognitive behavioral therapy and start an antidepressant. Which one of the following medications is approved by the FDA to treat depression in patients such as this? A) Esketamine nasal spray (Spravato) B) Bupropion C) Fluoxetine (Prozac) D) Venlafaxine
Reference: Miller L, Campo JV. Depression in adolescents. N Engl J Med. 2021;385(5):445-449.
Explanation: Two SSRIs, fluoxetine and escitalopram, have been approved by the FDA for the treatment of depression in adolescents. Other SSRIs such as sertraline, NDRIs such as bupropion, and SNRIs such as venlafaxine are often used off-label for this purpose but have not been approved by the FDA for use in adolescents. Esketamine is a ketamine nasal spray used in conjunction with an oral antidepressant for treatment-resistant depression in adults.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old patient presents with a 14-day history of a productive cough and green sputum. They report they initially had a fever but that it resolved after a few days. The cough is interfering with their sleep and daily activity, and they have tried cough drops without relief. They have a history of hypothyroidism for which they take levothyroxine (Synthroid), and do not have a history of asthma. They do not smoke, and their immunizations are up to date. On examination, the patient is afebrile and has an oxygen saturation of 98% on room air, a heart rate of 82 beats/min, and a blood pressure of 120/82 mm Hg. A lung examination reveals mild rhonchi that clear with cough, and you do not note any wheezing or egophony. Which one of the following treatments would be the most effective in reducing this patient's cough? A) An albuterol inhaler (Proventil, Ventolin) B) Amoxicillin/clavulanate (Augmentin) C) Azithromycin (Zithromax) D) Codeine/guaifenesin syrup E) Dextromethorphan (Delsym)
Reference: Killeen BM, Wolfson AB. Antibiotics for acute bronchitis. Am Fam Physician. 2020;102(9):Online.
Explanation: Acute bronchitis is a lower respiratory tract infection that is most commonly caused by a virus. Treatment is generally supportive as it is a self-limited illness. Guaifenesin, honey, and dextromethorphan have been shown to decrease the frequency of cough (SOR B). In otherwise healthy patients without evidence of pneumonia, acute bronchitis can last 2â€“3 weeks. Patients may exhibit purulent sputum, rhonchi, and wheezing with acute bronchitis but are typically afebrile with normal oxygen saturation. Î² -Agonists such as albuterol are not recommended for the treatment of acute bronchitis 2 unless wheezing is present (SOR B). While 90% of acute bronchitis cases are caused by viruses, a significant percentage are treated inappropriately with antibiotics. Antibiotics can decrease the duration of cough by 0.46 days, but the overall net benefit of antibiotics is negligible and often outweighed by potential side effects (SOR A). Studies show codeine has no benefit in the treatment of acute bronchitis and has rather significant side effects.

QuestionStem: A 20-year-old male presents to the urgent care center for left ankle pain and swelling following an ankle inversion injury that occurred a few hours earlier. A physical examination reveals mild swelling of the left ankle joint. Using the Ottawa foot and ankle rules, which one of the following findings, if present, would be an indication for radiography for an acute ankle inversion injury? A) Bone tenderness at the anterior edge of the lateral malleolus B) Bone tenderness at the anterior edge of the medial malleolus C) Bone tenderness at the base of the fourth metatarsal D) Bone tenderness of the navicular bone E) Inability to bear weight on the left foot for at least 10 steps after the injury
Reference: Mulhem E, Khondoker F, Kandiah S. Constipation in children and adolescents: evaluation and treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(5):469-478. | Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al: 2018 AHA/ACC/AACVPR/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/ADA/AGS/APhA/ ASPC/NLA/PCNA guideline on the management of blood cholesterol: A report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines. J Am Coll Cardiol 2019;73(24):e285-e350.
Explanation: When a foot or ankle fracture is suspected, a weight-bearing foot and ankle x-ray should be obtained. The Ottawa foot and ankle rules are a useful tool for determining the need for an x-ray for an acute ankle inversion injury. According to the Ottawa foot and ankle rules, a foot x-ray should be obtained if any of the following requirements are met: bone tenderness at the posterior edge or the tip of the lateral or medial malleolus, bone tenderness at the base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular bone, or inability to bear weight on the affected leg or foot for at least 4 steps after the injury.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 13-year-old male presents with his parents because they are concerned that he has very large-volume stools that clog the toilet. The patient says that he averages fewer than 2 bowel movements per week and they are sometimes painful. He does not have any abdominal pain and has not seen any blood in his stools. Examination findings are unremarkable. His height, weight, and BMI percentiles have remained steady for years. A CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, ferritin level, and TSH level are all normal. An abdominal x-ray is normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Polyethylene glycol (MiraLAX) daily B) Probiotics daily C) Magnetic resonance enterography D) Colonoscopy
Explanation: This patient meets 3 of the Rome IV diagnostic criteria for functional constipation (â‰¤2 bowel movements per week, painful hard stools, and stools that clog the toilet). There is good evidence that polyethylene glycol is effective for treating functional constipation in children and adolescents. There is no evidence that probiotics are effective. This patient does not have any red flag symptoms that would prompt a need for either magnetic resonance enterography to evaluate for inflammatory bowel disease or a colonoscopy to evaluate for a structural lesion.

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old male sees you for routine follow-up of diabetes mellitus. He smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and does not have any other medical conditions. His diabetes is well controlled on liraglutide (Victoza) and metformin. He has an LDL-cholesterol level of 120 mg/dL, an HDL-cholesterol level of 55 mg/dL, and a triglyceride level of 300 mg/dL. His 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk is 8.2%. According to 2018 guidelines from the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association, the most appropriate recommendation for this patient would be A) atorvastatin (Lipitor), 40 mg daily B) ezetimibe (Zetia), 10 mg daily C) fenofibrate, 160 mg daily D) pravastatin, 20 mg daily E) simvastatin (Zocor), 40 mg daily
Explanation: Patients 40â€“75 years of age with diabetes mellitus and an LDL-cholesterol level â‰¥70 mg/dL should be treated with a moderate-intensity statin without calculating a 10-year risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. However, high-intensity statin therapy should be considered to lower the LDL-cholesterol level by at least 50% for patients such as this one who are at elevated risk, especially those 50â€“75 years of age or with multiple risk factors. Of the options listed, atorvastatin, 40â€“80 mg daily, is the only therapy that is considered high-intensity statin therapy. Pravastatin, 20 mg daily, is low-intensity statin therapy. Simvastatin, 20â€“40 mg daily, is considered moderate-intensity therapy and is not recommended at doses >40 mg daily. Ezetimibe and fenofibrate are not recommended first-line medications for the treatment of hyperlipidemia and the prevention of cardiovascular disease.

QuestionStem: A 37-year-old male comes to the primary care clinic with a several-month history of worsening paresthesia in his right foot. He says that he sometimes has numbness and a hot sensation in his feet and has difficulty using the pedals of his car. Sometimes, he has pain that wakes him up at night but eases when he walks around the room. He has no significant past medical history but did sustain a severe right ankle sprain about 1 year ago. His vital signs are unremarkable. Examination of the ankle reveals pain along the medial malleolus and a positive Tinel sign at the ankle. An x-ray of the ankle is unremarkable. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Achilles tendinopathy B) Haglund deformity C) Heel pad syndrome D) Plantar fasciitis E) Tarsal tunnel syndrome
Reference: Tu P. Heel pain: diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2018;97(2):86-93.
Explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is an entrapment neuropathy of the foot caused by compression of the tibial nerve or one of its branches in the fibro-osseous tunnel under the flexor retinaculum and within the abductor hallucis muscle of the foot. It may occur following an injury, such as a fracture or sprain of the ankle. Patients can present with paresthesias, local pain behind the medial malleolus, and loss of sensation in the sole. Paresis of the small muscle of the foot can lead to impaired abduction. There is no criterion standard for the diagnosis of tarsal tunnel syndrome. The most helpful clinical diagnostic sign is a positive Tinel sign at the ankle, and electrophysiologic studies can help in making the diagnosis. MRI can be useful in cases of compression due to a mass lesion. Patients can begin a trial of conservative management, but advanced cases are better treated surgically. Achilles tendinopathy causes posterior heel pain that is localized at the site of insertion of the Achilles tendon and worsens with increased activity or pressure on the area. Haglund deformity presents with pain in the upper part of the posterior calcaneus and is more common in middle-aged women who repeatedly wear ill-fitting shoes. X-rays demonstrate the deformity on the superior aspect of the posterior calcaneus. Patients with heel pad syndrome report a deep-seated pain that feels like a bruise in the middle of the heel. The pain can be reproduced by palpating the area. It is typically an inflammatory response to damage of the heel pad secondary to walking barefoot for prolonged periods of time. Plantar fasciitis causes tenderness along the medial calcaneal tuberosity and along the plantar fascia. Patients typically report pain with their first steps after getting out of bed in the morning.

QuestionStem: A 36-year-old female comes to your office for a health maintenance examination. She mentions that she engages in amateur rifle target shooting matches. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate advice concerning this patientâ€™s physical safety? A) She should wear electronic hearing protection that amplifies quiet sounds B) She should wear noise-canceling headphones C) She should wear loose-fitting clothing D) She should wear her regular glasses so that she has eye protection
Explanation: Hearing protection is essential while shooting firearms because noise exposure often exceeds 140 dB. Even a single gunshot can cause permanent hearing loss. Earmuffs and earplugs with a noise reduction rate of 28â€“31 dB are common and inexpensive protective devices. Electronic hearing protection can amplify quiet sounds such as a range safety officer's instructions, but also safely protect against loud sounds. Many ranges require their use. Noise-canceling headphones do not provide enough noise reduction for gunshots. Wearing loose-fitting clothing can trap the ejected hot brass casings, leading to unsafe gun handling. Safety eyeglasses that meet the necessary certifications can protect against shrapnel from the bullet striking the target or a malfunction. Regular glasses do not provide sufficient eye protection.

QuestionStem: A 66-year-old male presents to your office with a 2-hour history of discomfort in the midsternal area that began while taking a walk earlier today. He says that he feels 90% better. He had a Welcome to Medicare visit 1 year ago, in which an EKG was normal. You order an EKG today. Which one of the following EKG findings, if present in this patient, should prompt urgent transport to the emergency department? A) First-degree atrioventricular block B) Frequent PVCs C) Left bundle branch block D) Sinus tachycardia E) Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy
Reference: McConaghy JR, Sharma M, Patel H: Acute chest pain in adults: Outpatient evaluation. Am Fam Physician 2020;102(12):721-727.
Explanation: Approximately 1% of primary care office visits are for evaluation of chest pain, in which 2%â€“4% of these patients have unstable angina or myocardial infarction. Twelve-lead EKG is recommended to look for ST-segment changes, new-onset left bundle branch block, presence of Q waves, and new T-wave inversions. The presence of new-onset left bundle branch block increases the likelihood of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and acute myocardial infarction. Patients with suspected ACS or the aforementioned changes on EKG should be transported immediately to the emergency department. The presence of first-degree atrioventricular block, frequent PVCs, sinus tachycardia, or voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy does not increase the likelihood of ACS.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 56-year-old female sees you for follow-up of pneumonia diagnosed in the emergency department (ED). A chest x-ray performed in the ED showed a left lower lobe infiltrate and several compression fractures involving the thoracic vertebrae. A DXA scan performed 7 months ago showed a T-score of â€“1.9. Her daily medications include a multivitamin; calcium, 1000 mg; and vitamin D (cholecalciferol), 800 IU. Today she feels 3 well and has no concerns. This patient would be most likely to benefit from therapy for which one of the following conditions? A) Osteopenia B) Osteoporosis C) Spondylolisthesis D) Spondylolysis
Explanation: Although this patient's T-score indicates osteopenia, the presence of fragility fractures indicates osteoporosis. The T-score is obtained by measuring bone mineral density using DXA at the spine, total hip, or femoral neck. A T-score between â€“1.0 and â€“2.49 indicates osteopenia, whereas a T-score of â‰¤â€“2.5 denotes osteoporosis. However, postmenopausal osteoporosis may be diagnosed by bone mineral density or by the presence of a fragility fracture, which is a fracture that occurs with no associated trauma or after a fall from standing height or lower. Hence, osteoporosis can be diagnosed and the patient further evaluated and treated even though her T-score is >â€“2.5. Spondylolisthesis is the acquired anterior displacement of one vertebral body over another and is typically associated with degenerative changes. Spondylolysis is a cause of acute back pain resulting from a stress fracture involving the pars interarticularis of the lumbar vertebral body. It is typically found in young athletes.

QuestionStem: A gravida 2 para 1 at 28 weeks' gestation presents with an episode of second-trimester bleeding that spontaneously resolved. She has not had any prenatal laboratory studies during either of her pregnancies. Ultrasonography today shows a healthy fetus and a small placental abruption. Which one of the following would be most important to order at this time? A) An antiphospholipid antibody profile B) An HIV test C) An Rh factor test D) A toxoplasmosis antibody test E) A urine toxicology screen for cocaine
Reference: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists’ Committee on Practice Bulletins—Gynecology. ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 194: polycystic ovary syndrome. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131(6):e157-e171.
Explanation: Blood type/Rh factor testing should be ordered when evaluating second-trimester bleeding. This patient would require a dose of Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) if found to be Rh negative. Therefore, of all the tests listed, an Rh factor test is the most time sensitive and affects the plan of care. Patients may have had unattended home births with prior pregnancies or refused laboratory tests altogether. With the potential exposure to mixing of maternal and fetal blood during the patient's first delivery, it is crucial during this pregnancy to ensure maternal and fetal blood are compatible because there is a high risk of contact between maternal and fetal blood. An antiphospholipid antibody profile would be appropriate for evaluation of an underlying hypercoagulable disorder, such as in cases of late pregnancy loss (>10 weeks), recurrent early pregnancy loss (<10 weeks), certain preterm deliveries, or thrombosis. Although HIV testing is an important component of prenatal laboratory studies, it would not be the most important test to order at this time. A toxoplasmosis antibody test may be considered if there are signs of infection or exposure to toxoplasmosis. While cocaine use can cause placental abruption, a urine toxicology screen for cocaine is not the most pressing laboratory study to order at this time.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 50-year-old nurse presents to your clinic 1 day after sustaining an accidental needlestick containing the blood of a person with active hepatitis B virus infection who has not yet begun treatment. Your patient completed the hepatitis B (HepB) vaccine series at age 30 before beginning his first nursing job. Hepatitis serologies drawn yesterday at his place of employment were positive for hepatitis B surface antibody (>10 mIU/mL). Which one of the following would be recommended for this patient? A) No additional vaccination B) Hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) only C) Recombinant HepB vaccine only D) Both HBIG and recombinant HepB vaccine
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Vitamin D Deficiency: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2014. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021.
Explanation: Postexposure prophylaxis with hepatitis B immune globulin or hepatitis B vaccine is not recommended in those who have completed the hepatitis B vaccine series and have proven immunity to hepatitis B virus. The presence of hepatitis B surface antibody titers of >10 mIU/mL are indicative of immunity either from previous vaccination or from previous infection with hepatitis B (both hepatitis B core and surface antibodies are reactive). Hepatitis B immune globulin would be recommended in a patient with negative hepatitis B serology testing.

QuestionStem: A 20-year-old female presents for a routine health maintenance examination and has no medical concerns today. She does not have a history of sexual activity, and screenings for alcohol and substance use, intimate partner violence, anxiety, depression, and suicidality are negative. An examination reveals fair skin, an elevated BMI of 31 kg/m2, and normal blood pressure. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, you should recommend screening for which one of the following? A) Cervical cancer B) Chlamydia C) Diabetes mellitus D) HIV E) Skin cancer
Explanation: This patient is a healthy young adult female who presents with an elevated BMI but no other identified risk factors. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends HIV screening for all persons 15â€“65 years of age (A recommendation). Cervical cancer screening is not recommended in women before age 21 (D recommendation), thus precluding the patient from screening. Chlamydia screening is recommended for all sexually active females under 25 years of age (B recommendation), but this patient has no history of sexual activity. Diabetes mellitus screening is recommended for persons 35â€“70 years of age with overweight or obesity (B recommendation). While the patient's age precludes her from this screening, she should be offered intensive multicomponent behavioral intervention for weight loss. Preventive counseling for skin cancer is recommended for persons with fair skin types who are 6 months to 24 years of age (B recommendation), but screening examinations are not recommended (I recommendation). This patient should also be offered screening for hepatitis C.

QuestionStem: A 65-year-old male presents to your clinic with symptoms of flashing lights and increased floaters in his left eye. He does not have any pain, and he has no past medical history. His vital signs are normal. His visual acuity is at baseline when wearing his glasses, and you note a visual field defect on the left. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Migraine with aura B) Central retinal artery occlusion C) Retinal detachment D) Globe rupture E) Acute ischemic stroke
Explanation: Family physicians frequently evaluate ophthalmologic symptoms and should be familiar with conditions requiring emergent evaluation. Peripheral flashing lights and floaters are common symptoms of retinal detachment. This patient should be referred for urgent ophthalmologic evaluation including a dilated fundoscopic examination. While positive and negative visual features are seen with migraine with aura, these features should be transient and followed by a headache typical of migraine. New-onset migraine in a 65-year-old is also uncommon and would require further evaluation. Central retinal artery occlusion, often ischemic or thromboembolic, typically occurs in those with risk factors such as diabetes mellitus, vascular disease, and tobacco use. Rapid, painless vision loss is often accompanied by afferent pupillary defect and the lesion can be visualized on a dilated fundoscopic examination as a cherry-red spot. Globe rupture is unlikely to occur without a history of trauma. Acute ischemic stroke may cause vision loss but is unlikely to cause ocular symptoms of flashing lights and floaters.

QuestionStem: A 46-year-old male presents for an acute visit due to a 2-week history of low back pain. He was moving furniture when the pain started, and he felt a pop in his right lower back area. The pain is severe enough that it keeps him from standing up straight and is causing him trouble with sleeping. He does not have fever or chills, numbness, paresthesia, or weakness in his lower extremities. He has no radicular pain on a straight legâ€“raising test. He went to a chiropractor who did x-rays and told him he needed to get an MRI. Which one of the following, if present, would be an indication for advanced imaging? A) Current cigarette use B) Current intravenous drug use C) Disrupted sleep due to nocturnal pain D) Restricted posture due to pain E) New urinary retention
Explanation: Early advanced imaging for low back pain increases the probability of surgery and is associated with a higher use of opioids, a higher cost of care, higher pain scores, and more work absenteeism. While there is no consensus on what constitutes red flags for low back pain, new urinary retention is concerning for cauda equina syndrome. Other symptoms associated with cauda equina syndrome are bladder fullness and saddle anesthesia. The severity of pain does not correlate clearly with pathologic back pain.
