While cigarette use increases the chance of an underlying cancer, without a known history of cancer or other signs, it is not an indication for advanced imaging. While intravenous drug use increases the risk of epidural abscess, with no other signs of infection or history of infection, it is not solitarily an indication for advanced imaging. Nocturnal pain is not uncommon in benign low back pain and may be associated with a more specific underlying cause if the pain occurs in the second part of the night (early morning).

QuestionStem: How long after intranasal administration does naloxone (Narcan) reach peak plasma concentrations? A) Immediately B) 30â€“60 seconds C) 3â€“5 minutes D) 15â€“30 minutes E) 1â€“2 hours
Explanation: After intranasal administration of naloxone, peak plasma concentrations are attained within 15â€“30 minutes, although its effect on the patient may be seen sooner. Absorption may be affected by substances or conditions that impact the nasal mucosa function or its blood flow. The half-life of naloxone is 2 hours, which is shorter than many long-acting opioids. Ongoing monitoring is important even after reversal of opioid respiratory depression is achieved.

QuestionStem: A 24-year-old male presents for evaluation of a bulge in his groin. He reports that it is larger at the end of the day and with straining but says it often goes away when he lies down. He reports a mild dull ache that limits his ability to lift lumber at work. On examination there is no obvious bulge in the inguinal area. When the patient bears down, a bulge becomes apparent. Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step? A) Inguinal ultrasonography B) Scrotal ultrasonography C) Abdominal and pelvic CT D) Physical therapy E) Surgical referral
Reference: Falk N, Cole A, Meredith TJ: Evaluation of suspected dementia. Am Fam Physician 2018;97(6):398-405. | Olsen KM, Manouchehr-Pour S, Donnelly EF, et al; Expert Panel on Thoracic Imaging; ACR Appropriateness Criteria: Hemoptysis. J Am Coll Radiol 2020;17(5S):S148-S159. | Searight HR: Counseling patients in primary care: Evidence-based strategies. Am Fam Physician 2018;98(12):719-728.
Explanation: This patient presents with an inguinal hernia that is apparent on examination. When the hernia is apparent, no further imaging is needed. Recent studies have found that nearly 50% of patients sent for inguinal imaging have been unnecessarily referred. When the hernia is symptomatic or limits function, surgical referral is appropriate. If the hernia is asymptomatic, then watchful waiting would be a viable option. Physical therapy would not be appropriate for this patient with a symptomatic inguinal hernia.

QuestionStem: A 72-year-old male presents to your office with his daughter who is concerned about her father's recent decline in memory. His history is notable for hypertension, urinary urgency with occasional incontinence, and GERD. On examination, you notice a slow, wide-based gait. A Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score is 22/30. MRI of the brain is notable for diffuse dilation of the lateral, third, and fourth ventricles, and the diagnosis is confirmed with a high-volume lumbar puncture. Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this condition? A) Carbidopa/levodopa (Sinemet), 12.5/50 mg daily B) Donepezil (Aricept), 5 mg daily C) Levothyroxine (Synthroid), 50 Î¼g daily D) Ventriculoperitoneal shunt E) Surgery with postoperative chemotherapy
Explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad symptoms associated with normal-pressure hydrocephalus: gait change, urinary incontinence, and dementia. MRI will show evidence of hydrocephalus, and gait changes will often improve with high-volume removal of cerebrospinal fluid. Treatment involves placing a ventriculoperitoneal shunt to relieve hydrocephalus. Carbidopa/levodopa, donepezil, levothyroxine, and surgery with chemotherapy are all treatments for other causes of dementia including Parkinson disease with Lewy body dementia, Alzheimer dementia, hypothyroidism, and brain tumor or neoplasm, respectively.

QuestionStem: A 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department with an acute onset of bloody urine confirmed to be hematuria on urinalysis. He does not have any pain, fever, or recent trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension that is managed with lisinopril (Zestril). A physical examination is unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation? A) Repeating the urinalysis in 1 month B) Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week C) Ordering urine cytology D) Referring the patient for immediate cystoscopy and imaging studies
Explanation: The American College of Physicians recommends that all adults with gross hematuria be referred for further urologic evaluation, including cystoscopy and imaging, due to the high association with malignancy. The sensitivity and specificity of cystoscopy for detecting bladder cancer are high, with a sensitivity ranging from 87% to 100% and a specificity of 64% to 100%. The positive predictive value of gross hematuria for urologic malignancy in older men is high. Gross hematuria warrants immediate investigation to rule out serious underlying conditions; thus a repeat urinalysis in 1 month would not be appropriate. Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week would not be appropriate without evidence of infection. Urine cytology has limited sensitivity and specificity and should not replace cystoscopy and imaging for the initial evaluation of hematuria.

QuestionStem: A 40-year-old male presents to establish care. He smokes 1Â½ packs of cigarettes per day and is uncertain about cutting back or quitting. While planning to enact a patient-centered strategy to help him overcome his reluctance, you recall that evidence supports the application of a counseling technique that uses open-ended questions, affirmations, reflective listening, and summary statements to help foster a sense of patient autonomy and effect positive behavioral change. Which one of the following counseling strategies best fits this description? A) BATHE (background, affect, troubles, handling, empathy) B) The five Aâ€™s (ask, advise, assess, assist, arrange) C) Motivational interviewing D) Transtheoretical (stages of change) model
Explanation: Motivational interviewing (MI) is the counseling strategy that best fits the described approach. MI is an evidence-proven strategy that is particularly helpful in situations of ambivalence toward change. In primary care settings, MI has been shown to help people achieve weight loss, improve blood pressure, and reduce alcohol use (SOR A). The key elements of MI include establishing rapport, eliciting the patient's values, asking open- ended questions, offering affirmations, practicing reflective listening, and recapping discussions with summarizing statements. The acronym OARS (open-ended questions, affirmations, reflective listening, summaries) serves as a reminder of these 4 MI components. The BATHE (background, affect, troubles, handling, empathy) model provides a structure for addressing various psychosocial problems. BATHE uses more targeted lines of questioning, in contrast to the MI approach of asking open-ended questions that give way to affirming, reflective, and summarizing statements. The five A's (ask, advise, assess, assist, arrange) approach provides a stepwise guide for assessing and counseling on health-related behaviors. In this strategy, the lines of questioning are targeted, rather than open-ended, leading to more directive recommendations and focused follow-up questions. The transtheoretical (stages of change) model can be helpful when patients are ambivalent, but this approach focuses more on identifying the patient's current level of readiness to modify a particular health behavior. The stages are precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, and maintenance. Identifying a patient's current stage can facilitate movement to the next stage of change and can provide a helpful framework to select another counseling strategy, such as MI.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old female with type 1 diabetes presents with a skin lesion on her leg. The lesion began as a small, dusky red, elevated nodule with a sharply circumscribed border, and slowly enlarged to become the lesion shown below. The most likely diagnosis is A) diabetic dermopathy B) diabetic lipoatrophy C) diabetic rubeosis D) eruptive xanthoma E) necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum
Reference: Loscalzo J, Fauci AS, Kasper DL, Hauser SL, Longo DL, Jameson JL, eds. Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw Hill; 2022:3120-3128.
Explanation: This patient's most likely diagnosis is necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. Its pathophysiology is poorly understood, but lesions are commonly found on the anterior and lateral surfaces of the lower legs. Lesions may begin as a small, dusky red, elevated nodule with a sharply circumscribed border. The nodule slowly enlarges, develops an irregular outline, and flattens, and has a brownish-yellow center. A reddened border may be present. Diabetic dermopathy characteristically occurs as crops of brownish atrophic lesions. In diabetic lipoatrophy the skin is depressed but otherwise normal. Diabetic rubeosis is described as reddening of the face and extremities in patients with long- standing diabetes mellitus. Eruptive xanthomas from diabetes are typically small, reddish- yellow nodules up to 0.5 cm in diameter that appear in clusters on the extensor surfaces and buttocks.

QuestionStem: Metformin can cause which one of the following? A) Chromatopsia B) Ileus C) Renal failure D) Vitamin B deficiency 12 E) Weight gain
Explanation: Asymptomatic vitamin B deficiency has been reported in a significant proportion of 12 patients taking metformin (SOR A). A randomized, placebo-controlled trial showed that patients taking metformin had a 19% decrease in vitamin B levels and a 7% increase in 12 absolute risk of vitamin B deficiency compared to the placebo group. 12 Chromatopsia, which is a visual disturbance of color perception, is associated with use of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, such as sildenafil. Metformin use is not associated with ileus. Although metformin use is contraindicated in patients with impaired renal function, metformin itself does not cause renal failure. Because diarrhea occurs relatively frequently with metformin therapy, metformin use is not commonly associated with weight gain.

QuestionStem: A 3-year-old female is brought to your office by her parents with fever, cough, and decreased appetite. On examination you hear crackles in the left upper lobe and a chest x-ray confirms a left upper lobe consolidation. She appears well hydrated and her vital signs are normal. On examination she is drinking water and interacting normally. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient's condition? A) Amoxicillin B) Azithromycin (Zithromax) C) Cefdinir D) Doxycycline E) Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim
Reference: Smith DK, Kuckel DP, Recidoro AM. Community-acquired pneumonia in children: rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician. 2021;104(6):618-625.
Explanation: The majority of cases of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in children are caused by viruses. If bacterial pneumonia were suspected, then antibiotics would be warranted. Given this patient's focal lung examination and x-ray findings, the most likely causative bacteria would be a typical pathogen such as Streptococcus pneumoniae or Haemophilus influenzae. The first-line choice of antibiotics would be amoxicillin for 7 days. Azithromycin is appropriate for treatment of atypical pneumonia. While cefdinir does treat the typical pathogens seen in CAP, it would not be recommended for first-line treatment due to its broader spectrum. Doxycycline is used to treat CAP but should not be used in a 3-year-old. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is not indicated for CAP treatment.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with pain in his right index finger after jamming it during basketball practice. An examination demonstrates a gross deformity of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of the affected finger. There is dorsal angulation of the proximal phalanx. Capillary refill, sensation, and pulses are all intact. Because the patient has pain with attempted movement of the finger, a digital block using 1% lidocaine without epinephrine is used to complete the examination. Complete x-rays, including true lateral films, reveal a very small fracture of the middle phalanx involving a minimal (<20%) area of the articular surface. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the treatment of this patient's finger dislocation? A) Discharging home, and repeating x-rays in 1 week B) Reduction, followed by splinting in a flexed position and buddy taping C) MRI of the finger D) Referral for physical therapy with instructions to start early range-of-motion exercises E) Urgent referral to an orthopedist for hand surgery
Reference: Leggit JC, Meko CJ. Acute finger injuries: part I. Tendons and ligaments. Am Fam Physician. 2006;73(5):810-816. | Borchers JR, Best TM: Common finger fractures and dislocations. Am Fam Physician 2012;85(8):805-810.
Explanation: This patient has a dorsal dislocation of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of the finger, which is the most commonly dislocated joint in the body. Examination of the patient's finger reveals intact neurovascular findings. Conservative management is indicated given the limited articular surface involvement. A simple, stable dorsal PIP dislocation can be managed with in-office reduction, followed by splinting in approximately 30Â° of flexion for 2â€“4 weeks. Buddy taping is also recommended to prevent hyperextension deformities. X- rays should be repeated directly after reduction, and again in 1 week. Neither MRI nor physical therapy are indicated. Immediate surgical referral is necessary for finger fracture-dislocations that are irreducible, unstable after reduction, or involve significant soft tissue, tendon, or neurovascular injury. Additional indications include fractures involving a large portion of the articular surface (typically >30%), significant displacement or angulation (>15Â°), malrotation, comminution, and persistent subluxation or dislocation on imaging. Open fractures (exposed bone or joint) and injuries with vascular compromise or loss of function also require urgent surgical evaluation.

QuestionStem: A 27-year-old female comes to your office because she is having moderately severe, diffuse abdominal pain with nausea and approximately 5 loose, greasy stools daily. She recently returned from a 2-week backpacking trip in Nicaragua. Stool studies are positive for Giardia. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment? A) Supportive care only B) Azithromycin (Zithromax) C) Ciprofloxacin (Cipro) D) Ivermectin (Stromectol) E) Metronidazole (Flagyl)
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Hepatitis C Virus Infection in Adolescents and Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2020. | Final Recommendation Statement: Elevated Blood Lead Levels in Children and Pregnant Women: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2019. | Peixoto AJ: Acute severe hypertension. N Engl J Med 2019;381(19):1843-1852.
Explanation: Giardia is a parasite that causes diarrheal illness. While found globally, it is especially common in areas where sanitary conditions are poor and water treatment facilities are limited. Transmission may be waterborne, foodborne, or fecal-oral. The incubation period is usually 1â€“2 weeks, and symptoms include greasy diarrhea, abdominal pain, and nausea. Fever and vomiting are uncommon. While some infections are self-limited, treatment is recommended for symptomatic individuals to improve their symptoms and prevent transmission to others. Metronidazole, nitazoxanide, or tinidazole are recommended for treatment of Giardia infection. Azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, and ivermectin are antimicrobials that are commonly used to treat other causes of traveler's diarrhea.

QuestionStem: A 49-year-old female with a past medical history of epilepsy presents to your office because of sadness, sleep disruption, generalized anhedonia, and a recent increase in appetite. Her Patient Health Questionnaireâ€“9 (PHQ-9) score is elevated at 14. Which one of the following medications should be AVOIDED in this patient? A) Bupropion B) Duloxetine (Cymbalta) C) Fluoxetine (Prozac) D) Mirtazapine (Remeron) E) Sertraline (Zoloft)
Explanation: Antidepressants including bupropion, trazodone, and tricyclic antidepressants are associated with an increased risk of seizure and should be avoided in patients with epilepsy. In general, SSRIs or SNRIs are safer choices. Mirtazapine and vortioxetine would also be preferred choices.

QuestionStem: A 20-year-old female presents for a health maintenance examination. She has no significant past medical history and no concerns. Based on U.S. Preventive Services Task Force guidelines, screening for which one of the following would be most appropriate at this time? A) Hepatitis C B) Hyperlipidemia C) Cervical cancer D) Skin cancer
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force gives a B recommendation for screening for hepatitis C in individuals 18â€“79 years of age. Cervical cancer screening is not recommended until at least age 21 (A recommendation). Evidence for screening for hyperlipidemia and skin cancer is uncertain. Current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of the benefits and harms of visual skin examination to screen for skin cancer (I recommendation), but counseling for ultraviolet exposure is recommended for persons 6 months to 24 years of age with fair skin types (B recommendation).

QuestionStem: A 75-year-old male with acute confusion presents to the emergency department and is found to have a blood pressure of 280/140 mm Hg. Which one of the following potential etiologies would provide the strongest indication for lowering his systolic blood pressure to a target of 120 mm Hg within 20 minutes? A) Acute aortic dissection B) Acute ischemic stroke C) Acute renal failure D) Hypertensive encephalopathy E) Intracerebral hemorrhage
Explanation: Treatment of acute aortic dissection includes aggressive lowering of the systolic blood pressure to 100â€“120 mm Hg within 20 minutes and concomitant lowering of the pulse rate to <60 beats/min to reduce shear stress on the aortic wall, minimize the risk of propagating the dissection, and reduce the risk of other complications, including rupture and aneurysmal degeneration. The optimal treatment of acute ischemic stroke, acute renal failure, hypertensive encephalopathy, and intracerebral hemorrhage requires blood pressure to be lowered more cautiously to avoid the risk of complications that may be seen with overly rapid and aggressive blood pressureâ€“lowering.

QuestionStem: A 56-year-old male sees you because he has been falling asleep at work in the afternoon. Upon questioning he notes that he is very tired all the time. Although he is in bed 8 hours per night and wakes with an alarm, he gets up 3 times nightly to urinate. He says that he has been told he snores. He does not have any constipation, dry skin, mood changes, stomach pain, changes in urine stream, shortness of breath, or chest pain. The patient is 173 cm (68 in) tall and weighs 95 kg (209 lb). His vital signs include a blood pressure of 140/88 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 79 beats/min, and a respiratory rate of 16/min. Which one of the following is most likely to yield the correct diagnosis? A) A serum TSH level B) Vitamin B and folate levels 12 C) Iron studies D) A comprehensive metabolic panel E) Polysomnography
Reference: Semelka M, Wilson J, Floyd R. Diagnosis and treatment of obstructive sleep apnea in adults. Am Fam Physician. 2016;94(5):355-360.
Explanation: The combination of obesity, loud snoring, and fatigue is suspicious for obstructive sleep apnea, and polysomnography is the definitive diagnostic study. A TSH level would be useful if hypothyroidism were suspected as the cause of this patient's fatigue, but it is less likely because he does not have any mood changes, dry skin, or constipation. Low vitamin B , folate, and iron levels are also less likely based on this patient's presentation. A 12 comprehensive metabolic panel would be useful if kidney or liver function were suspected as the cause of the patient's fatigue, but these are less likely because he does not have shortness of breath, stomach pain, or chest pain.

QuestionStem: A 16-year-old female presents for a routine well child examination. She has never been sexually active and does not use illicit substances. Based on guidelines from the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, screening patients such as this for which one of the following has the strongest evidence of benefit? A) Anxiety B) Chlamydia and gonorrhea C) Eating disorders D) Elevated blood pressure E) Scoliosis
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Hepatitis C Virus Infection in Adolescents and Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2020. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2018. | Final Recommendation Statement: Hepatitis C Virus Infection in Adolescents and Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2020. | Final Recommendation Statement: Elevated Blood Lead Levels in Children and Pregnant Women: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2019. | Ramirez J: Adhesive capsulitis: Diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician 2019;99(5):297-300. | Matheson EM, Bragg SW, Blackwelder RS: Diabetes-related foot infections: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2021;104(4):386-394. | Patel H, Skok C, DeMarco A. Peripheral edema: evaluation and management in primary care. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(5):557-564.
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends screening for anxiety in children and adolescents 8â€“18 years of age (B recommendation). The USPSTF does recommend screening for chlamydia and gonorrhea for sexually active females in this patient's age group (B recommendation); however, this patient is not sexually active. Screening adolescents for eating disorders, elevated blood pressure, and scoliosis all have insufficient evidence to assess the benefits and harms (I recommendation).

QuestionStem: A 50-year-old female presents for evaluation of unilateral shoulder pain without a history of acute injury. She has tried ibuprofen with minimal symptomatic relief. Plain radiographs of the affected shoulder taken at an urgent care clinic last week demonstrated no abnormal findings. Which one of the following physical examination findings would best support a diagnosis of adhesive capsulitis as the primary etiology of this patient's shoulder pain? A) Apprehension in a 90Â° position with abduction and external rotation B) Crepitus on passive abduction with preserved passive range of abduction C) Restricted active range of abduction with preserved passive range of abduction D) Restricted passive range of abduction and external rotation E) Weakness on resisted abduction and external rotation
Explanation: The hallmark finding of adhesive capsulitis, also known as frozen shoulder, is pain associated with restricted passive range of motion, particularly in external rotation, flexion, and abduction. Osteoarthritis of the glenohumeral joint also commonly causes pain and stiffness with limited passive range of motion; however, radiographs would show evidence of osteoarthritis, with joint space narrowing, sclerosis, osteophyte formation, and subchondral cystic change. With adhesive capsulitis, radiographs are typically normal, although characteristic findings are seen on MRI. Limited passive range of motion occurs with adhesive capsulitis and osteoarthritis because both conditions involve structural changes within the intra-articular space of the glenohumeral joint, thus resulting in mechanical restriction of the range of motion. Apprehension in a position of abduction and external rotation would suggest a diagnosis of shoulder instability. Crepitus on passive range of motion would suggest a mechanical abnormality within the shoulder girdle (either intra- or extra-articular) and is nonspecific to any one condition. Limited active range of motion in abduction with preserved passive range of motion is common to extra-articular sources of shoulder pain, such as rotator cuff tendinitis and impingement, subacromial bursitis, biceps tendinitis, and partial or complete rotator cuff tears. Weakness on manual muscle testing in abduction and external rotation would raise concern for a high-grade or full-thickness rotator cuff tear.

QuestionStem: A 60-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes presents to your office because he is concerned about an ulcer on the bottom of his right foot. He is uncertain how long the ulcer has been present. He is afebrile and otherwise asymptomatic. His most recent hemoglobin A is 9.7% and was obtained 9 months ago. His medications include metformin, 2000 mg 1c daily, and insulin glargine (Lantus), 34 units nightly. He does not regularly check his blood glucose levels. On examination, the patient has an ulcer on the plantar surface of the right foot overlying the head of the fifth metatarsal, measuring 1.0 cm in diameter. The ulcer appears deep with erythema that extends approximately 2.5 cm from the margin of the ulcer. A probe-to-bone test is positive. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next test to evaluate for osteomyelitis? A) A WBC count B) A culture swab C) Transcutaneous oximetry D) Plain radiography
Explanation: In a patient with diabetes mellitus and suspected osteomyelitis of the foot, initial evaluation should include a probe-to-bone test, a serum inflammatory marker such as an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and plain radiography. Plain radiography is the recommended initial imaging modality in the diagnosis of osteomyelitis due to its low cost, safety, and availability. If a plain radiograph suggests osteomyelitis, advanced imaging may not be necessary. While an elevated WBC count can be used to grade the severity of a diabetes-related foot infection, it can be normal in about half of patients with a severe diabetes-related foot infection and is not helpful in the diagnosis of osteomyelitis. A highly elevated ESR, however, is suggestive of an osteomyelitis infection. Superficial wound cultures are poorly sensitive and specific for identifying diabetes-related wound organisms compared to deep tissue cultures and are not used in the assessment of diabetes-related foot infections or osteomyelitis. While evaluation of peripheral vasculature and oxygenation may be warranted in the management of diabetes-related foot infections and osteomyelitis, it does not contribute to the diagnosis of osteomyelitis.

QuestionStem: A 63-year-old female with hypertension sees you for routine follow-up. She has been bothered by swelling in her legs for the last several months. Her blood pressure is well controlled and she does not have any shortness of breath, cough, difficulty breathing while lying down, or pain in her legs when walking. She thinks the swelling is better earlier in the day. Her current medications include hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril (Zestril), estrogen, levothyroxine (Synthroid), and as-needed acetaminophen. A physical examination demonstrates mild pitting edema of both legs from the ankle to the distal third of each calf. There are 2+ pedal pulses and no skin changes. Examinations of the heart and lungs are normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Holding estrogen, and monitoring for improvement in peripheral edema B) Holding lisinopril, and monitoring for improvement in peripheral edema C) Bilateral ankle-brachial indices D) Bilateral venous compression ultrasonography E) Bilateral venous duplex ultrasonography with reflux
Explanation: Adverse medication effects should always be considered when evaluating new symptoms, such as the association between estrogen use and peripheral edema. This patient's age suggests that she is likely remote from menopause, which provides a reason to consider discontinuing estrogen therapy. Peripheral edema is not associated with ACE inhibitor use. In fact, combining an ACE inhibitor with a calcium channel blocker (CCB) may decrease the edema attributable to CCB use. Ankle-brachial indices are indicated for the evaluation of suspected arterial disease. Arterial disease typically presents as lower extremity pain during activity that improves with rest. Arterial disease may lead to decreased arterial pulses. Arterial disease should be ruled out in those at risk before starting compression therapy for venous disease. Venous compression ultrasonography is indicated when deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is suspected. DVT is unlikely in the presentation of chronic bilateral lower extremity edema. DVT should be considered in acute unilateral edema, and the Wells score should be used to determine if there is an intermediate or high risk. The combination of a low-risk Wells score and a negative D-dimer level safely rules out DVT. Venous duplex ultrasonography with reflux is helpful in evaluating for chronic venous insufficiency. While this patient may have chronic venous insufficiency, her current medication use could be causing her edema. This should be evaluated before pursuing additional diagnostic testing given her lack of alarm symptoms, such as signs of heart failure or lower extremity ulcerations.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following antibiotics would be recommended for the treatment of syphilis in a pregnant patient who has a history of penicillin allergy? A) Azithromycin (Zithromax) B) Ceftriaxone C) Doxycycline D) Penicillin G benzathine (Bicillin L-A) after desensitization
Explanation: Parenteral penicillin G benzathine is the only therapy with documented effectiveness for treating syphilis in a pregnant person. In pregnant patients with any stage of syphilis and a reported penicillin allergy, the CDC recommends desensitization followed by treatment with penicillin G benzathine. Azithromycin and erythromycin are insufficient to cure maternal infection or treat fetal infection. There is insufficient data to recommend cephalosporins such as ceftriaxone for the treatment of maternal infections and the prevention of congenital infections. Doxycycline and tetracycline are not recommended for use in the second and third trimesters.

QuestionStem: A 7-year-old male with no significant past medical history presents with his parents for a well child visit. His BMI has consistently been above the 80th percentile. His parents have both struggled with maintaining healthy weights. To help them promote a healthy lifestyle for their child, you should tell the parents which one of the following? A) An hour per day of moderate or vigorous physical activity is recommended starting at age 6 B) Calorie restriction alone is sufficient in maintaining a healthy weight C) Children should avoid video screenâ€“based physical activity D) Organic fruit juices can be provided to achieve the recommended daily servings of fruits and vegetables
Explanation: Anticipatory guidance for lifestyle management in children should incorporate multiple approaches that address nutrition, physical activity, and behaviors that contribute to possible energy imbalance. These recommendations include 60 minutes per day of moderate to vigorous physical activity for children 6â€“17 years of age. Calorie restriction is beneficial in reducing weight in adolescents when combined with additional strategies, such as physical activity and behavioral changes. While excessive video screen time has been associated with increased pediatric obesity, screen-based physical activity (eg, video games that require whole-body movement) can help reduce weight in children with overweight and obesity. Children should consume 5 servings of fruits and vegetables per day. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends against the use of fruit juices as a substitute for whole fruit due to its high calorie content.

QuestionStem: A 56-year-old female presents with numbness of her bilateral feet. She first became aware of this during a recent pedicure when she had difficulty feeling the nail technician work on her toes and the plantar surface of her feet. Since that time, she has noted a feeling of "walking on cotton gauze" and has started wearing slippers around her home. She had intense pruritus of her feet approximately 6 months ago that persisted for several weeks, but it was not accompanied by paresthesia. She is otherwise in good health and participates in an aerobics class 3 days per week. She takes lisinopril (Zestril), 10 mg daily, for essential hypertension. She does not have any other symptoms. On examination, her cranial nerves, muscle strength, and reflexes are normal. Sensation is diminished over the plantar surface and toes of both feet. She has 2+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally. Routine laboratory studies, including a CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, hemoglobin A , serum protein electrophoresis, and TSH, 1c vitamin B , and folate levels, are all in normal range. She is concerned that she may be 12 developing multiple sclerosis. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Gabapentin (Neurontin), 100 mg 3 times daily B) Vitamin B (pyridoxine), 50 mg daily 6 C) Lyme antibody testing D) Nerve conduction testing and electromyography (EMG) E) Brain MRI
Reference: Chamberlain R: Hip pain in adults: Evaluation and differential diagnosis. Am Fam Physician 2021;103(2):81-89.
Explanation: This patient demonstrates a symmetric pattern of paresthesia without red flag symptoms such as ataxia, double vision, or speech issues, which makes a lesion of the central nervous system or a nerve root less likely. Because the results of her routine laboratory studies are normal, electrodiagnostic testing would be the recommended next step. While gabapentin can be helpful in treating painful peripheral neuropathy, further workup is needed to determine the cause of this patient's symptoms. Lyme antibody testing and vitamin level testing plus supplementation may be helpful if electrodiagnostic testing exhibits an axonal pattern. Imaging of the brain or spine, such as with MRI, is not recommended in patients without central neurologic signs or asymmetry.

QuestionStem: A 56-year-old female presents for evaluation of right heel pain. She recently returned from a hiking trip during which she developed progressive heel discomfort. The patient's only medication is losartan (Cozaar), which she takes for hypertension. After a thorough examination, you diagnose Achilles tendinopathy. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in diagnosis and management? A) MRI for confirmation of diagnosis B) Nonâ€“weight-bearing until pain is improved C) Corticosteroid injection of the Achilles tendon D) Platelet-rich plasma injection E) Referral for physical therapy
Reference: Arnold MJ, Moody AL: Common running injuries: Evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician 2018;97(8):510-516. | Burgers K, Lindberg B, Bevis ZJ: Chronic diarrhea in adults: Evaluation and differential diagnosis. Am Fam Physician 2020;101(8):472-480. | Bhasin S, Brito JP, Cunningham GR, et al. Testosterone therapy in men with hypogonadism: an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2018;103(5):1715-1744.
Explanation: Achilles tendinopathy is a common injury in active individuals. The diagnosis is made clinically; therefore, additional imaging is not indicated unless there is concern for an alternate diagnosis. Current recommendations for management include physical therapy for activity modification and targeted exercises. Neither complete rest nor corticosteroid injection are currently recommended for management of Achilles tendinopathy. While platelet-rich plasma injection continues to be studied in tendon injuries, it is not currently recommended as first-line management. Furthermore, studies remain mixed as to whether platelet-rich plasma injections are superior to placebo for pain control and return to normal activity.

QuestionStem: A 45-year-old male with a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes is anxious about managing his condition and uncertain about his ability to meet the demands of treatment and self-care. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate strategy to help him understand his diagnosis and follow through on lifestyle and/or medication changes? A) Emphasizing the seriousness of the condition by showing pictures of patients with complications B) Explaining the diagnosis and treatment plan with medical language to ensure professional communication C) Supplying only detailed written instructions about his condition immediately after the appointment D) Using motivational interviewing to identify what is most important to the patient and create impetus for change
Explanation: Motivational interviewing is the communication strategy with the best evidence of leading to a change in behavior. Scare tactics have not been shown to be effective. Physicians should speak in language that the patient can understand rather than use jargon. Supplying too much detail may overwhelm the patient, especially if it is not tailored to the individual's goals.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 15-year-old female presents to your office with a history of frequent, generalized abdominal pain and loose stools daily for the last few months. She has not noted any relationship to food or a specific frequency. She has not had any previous surgeries and is up to date on routine vaccinations. At her 14-year-old well child visit, her height was at the 50th percentile and her weight was at the 60th percentile. Today, her height is at the 35th percentile and her weight at the 20th percentile. She has had a weight loss of 1 kg (2 lb). Her vital signs are normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Reassurance and dietary monitoring to evaluate for a relationship to symptoms B) Fecal fat testing C) An IgA tissue transglutaminase level D) Carbohydrate breath testing E) A referral for esophagogastroduodenoscopy
Explanation: Patients with classic symptoms of diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, bloating, malabsorption, and failure to thrive should undergo evaluation for celiac disease. Serologic testing for IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG) is the preferred initial screening in all age groups due to its high sensitivity (95%) and specificity (95%). When celiac disease is strongly suspected, total IgA levels should also be obtained because 2%â€“3% of these patients will have IgA deficiency. If IgA tTG serology is positive, most patients will require esophagogastroduodenoscopy with small-bowel biopsy for confirmation, but it is not used as an initial test and some organizations state that a no-biopsy technique is safe for children with very high IgA tTG levels. This patient has classic signs of malabsorption and unexplained weight loss, indicating need for further evaluation. Reassurance would be inappropriate at this time. Fecal fat testing may be useful in the workup of suspected malabsorption symptoms, but the initial test is usually a qualitative assessment of fat in a single specimen because it is less expensive and easier to perform than 72-hour testing. If high suspicion remains after negative initial testing, a 72-hour fecal fat test should be performed. Carbohydrate breath testing can be used to evaluate for suspected small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO). SIBO generally occurs in individuals with abnormal anatomy or other risk factors for abnormal intestinal mobility, including diabetes mellitus or scleroderma. The majority of patients with SIBO present with bloating and flatulence.

QuestionStem: A 60-year-old male asks you about testosterone supplementation during a routine office visit. He reports that he has had decreased energy, decreased libido, and loss of upper and lower body strength over the last year. He would like to have his testosterone level checked today. Which one of the following symptoms, if present in this patient, would be most concerning for testosterone deficiency? A) Difficulties with memory B) Fatigue or diminished physical stamina C) Loss of axillary and pubic hair D) Thinning of hair at the temples E) Thinning or loss of eyebrows
Explanation: Loss of axillary and pubic hair is one of the more specific symptoms of male hypogonadism. Memory issues have myriad etiologies; they may be secondary to attention disorders, mood disorders, sleep apnea, or other neurologic disorders. Fatigue or diminished stamina may be a symptom of male hypogonadism but also can be indicative of other endocrine, musculoskeletal, or rheumatologic disorders. Thinning hair at the temples may occur with aging and with androgenic alopecia. Thinning of the eyebrows may be related to thyroid disorders.

QuestionStem: A 3-year-old female is brought to your office by her parents for a preschool examination. She had a normal birth and is up to date on her vaccinations. On examination she appears to be healthy, and her development is appropriate for her age. She is in the 50th percentile for height and weight. You note a grade 1â€“2/6 continuous murmur best heard in the infraclavicular region when she is upright. The murmur changes slightly in intensity with head movement and is absent when she is supine. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Reassuring the parents that this is a benign venous hum that needs no follow-up B) Scheduling a follow-up appointment in 1 month to recheck the murmur C) Recommending that the patient undergo echocardiography when she reaches adulthood D) Sending the patient for evaluation for repair of a patent ductus arteriosus E) Referral to a pediatric cardiologist
Reference: Ford B, Lara S, Park J. Heart murmurs in children: evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(3):250-261. | Pflipsen MC, Vega Colon KM. Anaphylaxis: recognition and management. Am Fam Physician. 2020;102(6):355-362. | Douketis JD, Spyropoulos AC, Murad MH, et al. Perioperative management of antithrombotic therapy: an American College of Chest Physicians clinical practice guideline. Chest. 2022;162(5):e207-e243.
Explanation: A venous hum is an innocent heart murmur that is caused by vein vibration. It is commonly heard in children and may appear between 3 and 6 years of age. It is typically audible only when the child is upright, and changes with head movement. In a child with a venous hum, reassurance of the parents is appropriate, and no follow-up is necessary.

QuestionStem: A 40-year-old female presents to your office after an episode of anaphylaxis. She has a known tree nut allergy and developed oral swelling, dyspnea, and chest pain after eating a muffin from a coffee shop. Her symptoms initially improved after she used her epinephrine auto-injector but began to worsen 10 minutes later. She used a second dose of her auto-injector 10 minutes prior to presentation and is feeling much better. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Starting famotidine (Pepcid), 20 mg daily, and follow-up in 1 week B) Starting prednisone, 40 mg for 5 days, and follow-up in 5 days C) Starting fexofenadine (Allegra), 180 mg daily, and immediate follow-up with an allergist D) Transfer to the emergency department (ED) with observation for 1â€“2 hours prior to discharge E) Transfer to the ED with observation for 6â€“12 hours prior to discharge
Explanation: This patient experienced anaphylaxis due to her tree nut allergy. Her anaphylaxis was refractory and required 2 doses of epinephrine for treatment. Although she is feeling better, patients with refractory anaphylaxis should be transferred to a higher level of care, such as an emergency department, for further evaluation and management. Patients who have had severe or refractory anaphylaxis are at risk for a biphasic reaction and should be observed for 6â€“12 hours before discharge. Although medications such as H - and H -blockers and corticosteroids can be helpful in 1 2 the postanaphylaxis care of patients, they are not the mainstay of therapy for patients who have had severe anaphylaxis.

QuestionStem: A 42-year-old male has been admitted to the hospital for hypotension and electrolyte disturbances due to chronic diarrhea. He was stabilized in the ICU and transferred to the inpatient floor. His gastroenterologist is asking for a colonoscopy with biopsies. His medical history is significant for congenital heart disease with a mechanical aortic valve replacement, an embolic stroke resulting in right hemiparesis, a deep vein thrombosis, and a solitary kidney. He is currently anticoagulated with warfarin and has an INR of 2.6. He has a creatinine level of 2.8 mg/dL (N 0.7â€“1.3) and a glomerular filtration rate of 28 mL/min/1.73 m2. In addition to holding warfarin for the planned colonoscopy, how should you manage this patientâ€™s anticoagulation while he is hospitalized? A) Resuming warfarin post procedure with a goal INR of 2.0â€“3.0 B) Vitamin K for reversal, and resuming warfarin post procedure with a goal INR of 2.5â€“3.5 C) Enoxaparin (Lovenox), 1.5 mg/kg daily D) Heparin, 5000 units subcutaneously 3 times daily E) A heparin drip titrated to achieve therapeutic aPTT or anti-factor Xa levels
Explanation: This patient requires a procedure with a high likelihood of bleeding and should not be anticoagulated at the time of the procedure. He also has a high likelihood of thromboembolic disease and should receive bridging with a heparin drip until his INR has normalized for the planned procedure. With his mechanical heart valve, the correct INR goal is 2.5â€“3.5. Reversal with vitamin K is not necessary because he is stable and not actively bleeding. He is not a candidate for enoxaparin due to his glomerular filtration rate. Heparin, 5000 units subcutaneously, is a prophylactic dose, not a treatment dose.

QuestionStem: A 4-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his parents after tripping and falling on his hand in an outstretched position. An x-ray reveals a buckle (torus) fracture of the distal radial metaphysis. Which one of the following management strategies would be most appropriate? A) A short arm splint for 3 weeks with follow-up radiography B) A short arm splint for 3 weeks with follow-up radiography only if pain or tenderness persists C) A short arm cast for 6 weeks with follow-up radiography D) A short arm cast for 6 weeks with follow-up radiography only if pain or tenderness persists
Reference: Patel DS, Statuta SM, Ahmed N: Common fractures of the radius and ulna. Am Fam Physician 2021;103(6):345-354.
Explanation: Buckle (torus) fractures are incomplete compression fractures that cause cortical bulging of the bone without cortical disruption. They are common in children because of the elasticity of their bones. Distal radius buckle fractures should be treated with short arm immobilization using a short arm splint, soft cast, or wrap for 3 weeks. Complete immobilization with a cast is unnecessary. According to the Choosing Wisely campaign, follow-up radiography is unnecessary unless the patient continues to experience pain or tenderness.

QuestionStem: A 19-year-old female presents for a wellness visit while home on college break. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following screenings is indicated? A) Cavities or periodontal disease B) Dyslipidemia C) Skin cancer D) Suicide risk E) Unhealthy drug use
Reference: Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Perinatal Depression: Preventive Interventions. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2019. | Lipman GS, Eifling KP, Ellis MA, et al: Wilderness Medical Society practice guidelines for the prevention and treatment of heat-related illness. Wilderness Environ Med 2013;24(4):351-361.
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends screening for unhealthy drug use in adults â‰¥18 years of age (B recommendation). Unhealthy drug use is defined as using psychoactive medications for purposes other than as prescribed or illegally obtaining drugs for personal use. For this patient's age group, the USPSTF has determined the evidence to be insufficient to recommend for or against screening for cavities or periodontal disease, dyslipidemia, skin cancer, or suicide risk (I recommendation).

QuestionStem: A 33-year-old oyster fisherman presents because of a 3-day history of diarrhea. He describes frequent, large-volume, watery stool that is milky white. His vital signs are normal and he appears nontoxic. Infection with which one of the following pathogens is most likely? A) Clostridioides difficile B) Enteropathogenic Escherichia coli C) Enterotoxigenic E coli D) Vibrio species E) Yersinia enterocolitica
Explanation: In patients presenting with large-volume rice-water or milky-white stools, the most likely pathogen is Vibrio species. Patients with this presentation should be tested for infection regardless of exposure to undercooked shellfish or brackish waters. Guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America recommend testing for infection with Clostridioides difficile, enteropathogenic Escherichia coli, enterotoxigenic E coli, and Yersinia enterocolitica in patients with diarrhea and fever, bloody or mucoid stools, signs of sepsis, or severe abdominal cramping or tenderness.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old female with Down syndrome presents for a health maintenance examination. She does not have any new symptoms or concerns today. Her only medication is an etonogestrel implant (Nexplanon) that was placed 2 years ago. She is monogamous with a male sex partner. On examination you note normal vital signs with a BMI of 28 kg/m2. At her most recent laboratory evaluation 2 years ago, she had an LDL-cholesterol level of 104 mg/dL, a fasting glucose level of 89 mg/dL, and a TSH level of 2.67 Î¼U/mL (N 0.4â€“4.2). Which one of the following screening evaluations should you order today? A) The National Task Groupâ€“Early Detection Screen for Dementia tool B) A fasting lipid panel C) TSH testing D) A DXA scan E) Cervical spine radiography
Explanation: In 2017, the U.S. nonprofit group Global Down Syndrome Foundation convened a workgroup to create an evidence-based guideline for clinicians and adults with Down syndrome and their supporters. This guideline was published in 2020 and includes responses to 10 questions that were created using the Population/Intervention/Comparator/Outcome (PICO) format. They address screening for conditions known to be of higher prevalence in individuals with Down syndrome. In the United States, hypothyroidism is common in individuals with Down syndrome, with an estimated prevalence of approximately 50% in adults >30 years of age. Screening is therefore recommended with TSH testing every 1â€“2 years starting at age 21. This patient's TSH level was last checked 2 years ago and is due today. Dementia occurs on average at younger ages in those with Down syndrome than those without Down syndrome; however, it is rare before the age of 40. If any concerning symptoms arise in this age group, a thorough investigation for alternative causes is recommended. Routine screening for dementia is recommended beginning at age 40 for individuals with Down syndrome. A validated tool such as the National Task Groupâ€“Early Detection Screen for Dementia should be used. Cardiovascular disease prevention is recommended for individuals with Down syndrome per U.S. Preventive Services Task Force guidelines. This patient's LDL-cholesterol level was last checked 2 years ago and is not due for recheck today. The prevalence of osteoporosis in individuals with Down syndrome has been estimated with a wide degree of variation (1.4%â€“45.1%). There is no evidence that DXA screening at ages younger than recommended for adults without Down syndrome (age 65) is of benefit. Although atlantoaxial instability (AAI) is known to have a higher prevalence in those with Down syndrome than those without Down syndrome, no studies have assessed the use of cervical spine radiography for diagnosing AAI. Screening radiography is therefore not recommended; instead, it is recommended that clinicians remain alert for signs and symptoms of cervical myelopathy, such as incontinence, altered gait, brisk reflexes, and clonus.

QuestionStem: You are participating in a continuing medical education (CME) course at a Colorado ski resort that is 9600 ft (2926 m) above sea level. You and your travel companions have had intermittent issues with headaches, loss of appetite, and difficulty sleeping. On the third day, one of your companions starts to experience shortness of breath that is not relieved with rest. Their symptoms worsen throughout the day and they become slightly tachycardic. Later that evening, they develop a cough that produces frothy sputum. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step? A) Acetazolamide B) Dexamethasone C) Nifedipine D) A COVID-19 test E) Oxygen supplementation, and descending to a lower elevation
Explanation: The risk for acute mountain sickness increases when sleeping above altitudes of about 8200 ft (2499 m). Symptoms can include difficulty sleeping, headaches, and decreased appetite. This patient has more severe symptoms of high-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE), which is a medical emergency, and should begin supplemental oxygen and descend to a lower altitude if possible. Acetazolamide can be used to prevent acute mountain sickness, but it is not a treatment. Dexamethasone is a supplemental treatment for high-altitude cerebral edema. Nifedipine can be used in the prevention of HAPE and as a treatment in emergency situations in which descent or a hyperbaric chamber is not available. In this case, the patient is at a modern ski resort and descent should be feasible. COVID-19 infection is unlikely in this scenario.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following antibiotics is most commonly associated with Stevens-Johnson syndrome? A) Cephalexin B) Ciprofloxacin C) Fosfomycin D) Nitrofurantoin E) Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim
Explanation: Antibiotics are the most common cause of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis, accounting for more than one-quarter of cases worldwide. Sulfonamide antibiotics, such as sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, are associated with the majority of antibiotic-associated SJS cases worldwide. Antibiotic stewardship is always important, and consideration for both relatively benign and life-threatening side effects should be considered with every prescription written.

QuestionStem: During an annual visit, a 62-year-old female reports an episode of vaginal spotting that lasted for a few days in May. It is now July and she has not had another recurrence. A transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial stripe of 4 mm, no fibroids, and no polyps. Which one of the following would be most appropriate regarding follow-up evaluation? A) No further evaluation B) Endometrial biopsy C) Hysteroscopy and biopsy D) Dilation and curettage E) Repeat ultrasonography in 1 year
Explanation: Transvaginal ultrasonography or endometrial biopsy are both appropriate initial diagnostic tests for postmenopausal bleeding. An ultrasound showing an endometrial stripe â‰¤4 mm is associated with a >99% negative predictive value for endometrial cancer and no further evaluation is needed unless the bleeding is recurrent. If bleeding is persistent or recurrent, then a tissue sample would be appropriate. If an endometrial biopsy is not helpful and bleeding continues to persist or recur, then further evaluation with hysteroscopy and curettage would be indicated.

QuestionStem: A 25-year-old male presents with shoulder pain after having fallen on his arm in an outstretched position. He reports a sensation of catching and popping in the shoulder. With the patient seated and hands placed on his hips, you apply force to the elbow and direct the humerus anteriorly and superiorly, while asking the patient to resist your motion, which produces pain in the affected shoulder. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Acromioclavicular joint arthritis B) Adhesive capsulitis C) Glenohumeral osteoarthritis D) A glenoid labral tear E) A rotator cuff tear
Explanation: This patient's history of a catching or popping sensation combined with a positive anterior slide test, as described in the physical examination, is highly suggestive of a glenoid labral tear. This combination of history and the positive anterior slide test has a positive likelihood ratio of 6.0 in diagnosing a glenoid labral tear. Acromioclavicular joint arthritis presents with superior shoulder pain and tenderness over the acromioclavicular joint, often with a positive cross-body adduction test. Adhesive capsulitis is characterized by diffuse shoulder pain and restricted passive range of motion. Glenohumeral osteoarthritis usually presents with a gradual onset of pain and loss of motion, particularly in older patients. A rotator cuff tear typically presents with pain and weakness, especially with overhead activities, and positive findings on the empty can and external rotation tests. Acromioclavicular joint arthritis, adhesive capsulitis, glenohumeral osteoarthritis, and rotator cuff tears are not associated with catching or popping sensations, and the crank and anterior slide tests are not specific for these conditions.

QuestionStem: According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following is the recommended next step for adults who screen positive for unhealthy alcohol use? A) Brief counseling interventions B) Cognitive behavioral therapy C) Mindfulness-based therapy D) Interpersonal psychotherapy E) Referral for alcohol use disorder treatment
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening all adults for unhealthy alcohol use and implementing brief counseling interventions for those who screen positive (B recommendation). The screening, brief intervention, and referral to treatment (SBIRT) approach has been shown to decrease the number of patients with risky drinking after the intervention. Motivational interviewing is often used for brief counseling in primary care settings. Cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based therapy, and interpersonal psychotherapy are different psychotherapy approaches that are used as longer-term counseling methods and would not be appropriate for use as brief counseling interventions for adults with unhealthy alcohol use. Patients identified as having alcohol use disorder (AUD) by DSM-5 criteria should be referred for treatment.

QuestionStem: A 30-year-old female presents with constipation and severe anal pain following bowel movements. On examination, you observe an anal fissure. In addition to increasing fluids, fiber, and warm sitz baths, which one of the following would be effective and least likely to result in side effects? A) Topical calcium channel blockers B) Topical capsaicin C) Topical nitroglycerin D) Oral calcium channel blockers
Explanation: For anal fissures, topical sphincter relaxants applied externally around the anus 2â€“4 times per day help reduce sphincter tone and promote healing. Topical calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem are best tolerated. Topical nitroglycerin is also effective but is associated with more side effects such as headaches and lightheadedness, which results in 1 in 5 patients stopping therapy. Botulinum toxin is also effective. Topical capsaicin is an irritant. Oral calcium channel blockers are not effective for treatment of anal fissures.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following interventions lowers the risk of diverticulitis? A) Avoiding consumption of popcorn, nuts, and seeds B) Regular consumption of red meat C) A vegetarian diet D) Long-term NSAID use
Explanation: Diverticulitis is an inflammation of diverticula caused by a protrusion through the intestinal wall. Risk factors include constipation, a low-fiber diet, increasing age, red meat consumption, smoking, obesity, lack of exercise, aspirin and NSAID use, and genetic susceptibility. Consuming a vegetarian diet may help to prevent diverticulitis. Red meat intake, long-term NSAID use, and tobacco use are risk factors for diverticulitis. After years of research, it was concluded that consumption of nuts, seeds, and popcorn is not a risk factor for diverticulitis (SOR B). The incidence of diverticulitis is increasing, yet screening is not recommended. Tenderness located in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen is the most specific finding of diverticulitis. Evaluation includes a CBC, a basic metabolic panel, a C-reactive protein level, and urine analysis. CT with contrast is the imaging modality of choice if the diagnosis is uncertain. Treatment is based on severity and includes oral and intravenous antibiotics, bowel rest and a clear liquid diet, and surgical management for abscess formation or peritonitis as well as for prevention of recurrences.

QuestionStem: An 82-year-old male presents to your office because he has been experiencing severe, stabbing pain that feels like an electric shock on the left side of his face in his cheek, lips, and chin. The episodes are usually triggered by light touch or eating and last for a few seconds. Which one of the following cranial nerves is associated with this disorder? A) V B) VII C) X D) XI E) XII
Reference: Light RW. Disorders of the pleura. In: Loscalzo J, Fauci AS, Kasper DL, Hauser SL, Longo DL, Jameson JL, eds. Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw Hill; 2022:2197-2200. | Zakrzewska JM, Linskey ME: Trigeminal neuralgia. Am Fam Physician 2016;94(2):133-135.
Explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by severe paroxysms of pain in the distribution of 1 or more branches of cranial nerve V. Impairment of cranial nerve VII results in paralysis of facial muscles, such as Bell palsy. Impairment of cranial nerve X may cause dysphagia and dysphonia, cranial nerve XI involvement results in neck weakness, and cranial nerve XII damage leads to tongue paralysis.

QuestionStem: Which one of the following social determinants of health has a protective effective against Alzheimer disease and related types of dementia? A) Decreasing BMI with older age B) Early-life adversity C) Low education level D) Social engagement
Explanation: Social determinants of health (SDOH) are the conditions in which people are born, work, live, and age as well as the wider set of forces and systems that shape the conditions of daily life. Several SDOH affect cognition and risk of Alzheimer disease and related dementias (ADRDs). For example, higher levels of social engagement have a protective effect against ADRDs. Conversely, SDOH such as decreasing BMI with older age, early-life adversity, low education level, food insecurity, midlife obesity, manual labor, and low socioeconomic status are associated with a greater risk of ADRDs.

QuestionStem: A 72-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes comes to your office for evaluation of muscle aches and fatigue. His current medications include rosuvastatin (Crestor), dapagliflozin (Farxiga), and semaglutide (Ozempic). Over the past month he has noticed increasing pain in his hips, thighs, and shoulders without any apparent trigger. He feels so stiff in the morning that it is becoming difficult to get out of bed. On examination his strength is diminished (4/5) in the proximal muscle groups. You do not note any swelling or redness of any joints, muscles, or extremities. He has normal reflexes and no clonus. Laboratory Findings Hemoglobin A 6.4% 1c Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL (N 13.2â€“16.6) Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (N 0.7â€“1.3) Potassium 4.2 mEq/L (N 3.5â€“5.1) Creatine kinase 84 U/L (N 55â€“170) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 88 mm/hr (N 0â€“20) C-reactive protein 9.2 mg/L (N 0â€“0.3) Antinuclear antibody <1:40 (N <1:40) Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's myalgias? A) Fibromyalgia B) Polymyalgia rheumatica C) Polymyositis D) Statin-induced myopathy E) Systemic lupus erythematosus
Reference: Raleigh MF, Stoddard J, Darrow HJ. Polymyalgia rheumatica and giant cell arteritis: rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician 2022;106(4):420-426. | Final Recommendation Statement: Depression in Adults: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2016.
Explanation: These findings are concerning for polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR), an inflammatory condition that typically affects adults â‰¥50 years of age. This patient's proximal muscle pain, fatigue, and decreased proximal muscle strength are consistent with this diagnosis, as are the elevated inflammatory markers (erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level) and mild anemia. A normal creatine kinase level is expected in patients with PMR. PMR is diagnosed clinically and treated with oral glucocorticoids. Rapid improvement with therapy is expected; if improvement does not occur, an alternate diagnosis should be explored. Giant cell arteritis, also known as temporal arteritis, is associated with PMR. It causes temporal headaches, visual changes, and constitutional symptoms. Early identification and treatment are essential to prevent permanent vision change. Fibromyalgia does not cause elevated inflammatory markers. Polymyositis and statin- induced myopathy may cause myalgias but are typically associated with elevations in creatine kinase, which this patient does not have. Almost all patients with systemic lupus erythematosus have an elevated antinuclear antibody titer, which this patient also does not have.

QuestionStem: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends which one of the following regarding screening asymptomatic adolescents and adults for herpes simplex virus? A) No testing B) HSV-1 antibody testing C) HSV-2 antibody testing D) HSV-1 and HSV-2 antibody testing E) HSV-1 and HSV-2 antigen testing
Explanation: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force concluded with moderate certainty that the harms outweighed the benefits for routine, population-based serologic screening for infection with herpes simplex virus (HSV) in asymptomatic adolescents and adults, including pregnant patients (D recommendation). High false-positive rates are common in asymptomatic patients who undergo serologic testing for infection with HSV. In addition, treatment is not recommended in asymptomatic patients with a positive test.

QuestionStem: A 2-year-old child is brought to the urgent care clinic by their parents with a 2-day history of abdominal pain and vomiting. The child has been irritable over the past 2 days with intermittent abdominal pain and several episodes of associated emesis, and just had a bloody stool that the parents describe as resembling jelly. The child appears uncomfortable but is alert and afebrile, with normal blood pressure. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A) Intussusception B) Necrotizing enterocolitis C) Pyloric stenosis D) Volvulus
Explanation: Intussusception is a condition in which a part of the intestine slides or "telescopes" into an adjacent section. It presents with a classic triad of intermittent abdominal pain, emesis, and blood-tinged mucous stools that are sometimes called "red currant jelly" stools. It most commonly occurs at the ileocolic junction and typically affects infants and toddlers. The initial evaluation begins with ultrasonography. An enema with air or contrast can both confirm the diagnosis and reverse the intussusception. If this procedure does not relieve the condition, surgical correction is necessary. Necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe intestinal infection that typically presents with severe abdominal pain, lethargy, and unstable vital signs. Pyloric stenosis is notable for projectile vomiting, but not bloody stools. Volvulus typically involves a sudden onset of vomiting and abdominal pain and even distension, but without bloody stools.

QuestionStem: A generally healthy 54-year-old male with a 20-pack-year smoking history presents to discuss smoking cessation. His first grandchild was just born and he is eager for help in quitting cigarette smoking. He quit smoking 2 years ago using nicotine gum (Nicorette Gum), but resumed smoking 5 months later. He has also explored telephone helplines and support groups, but did not find them beneficial. He currently smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day. Which one of the following would be most likely to help this patient quit smoking? A) Nicotine gum only B) Extended-release bupropion (Wellbutrin SR) only C) Varenicline only D) Nicotine gum plus extended-release bupropion E) Nicotine gum plus varenicline
Reference: Gaddey HL, Dakkak M, Jackson NM. Smoking cessation interventions. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(5):513-522. | Fontenelle LF, Sarti TD. Kidney stones: treatment and prevention. Am Fam Physician. 2019;99(8):490-496. | Braun MM, Barstow CH, Pyzocha NJ. Diagnosis and management of sodium disorders: hyponatremia and hypernatremia. Am Fam Physician. 2015;91(5):299-307.
Explanation: Seven pharmacotherapies are approved by the FDA for smoking cessation, including bupropion, varenicline, and 5 forms of nicotine replacement therapy (NRT). All of these have been shown to be beneficial in smoking cessation compared to placebo. Compared to NRT and bupropion, varenicline appears to be the most effective monotherapy with a number needed to treat of 7. Recent meta-analyses suggest that a combination of pharmacotherapies is more effective than monotherapy. Varenicline plus NRT is more likely to result in sustained smoking cessation compared to bupropion plus NRT.

QuestionStem: A 60-year-old female returns to discuss management of her recurrent nephrolithiasis. She has a history of calcium stones and just recently passed her fourth stone this year despite appropriate lifestyle changes, including a fluid intake of 3 L per day and a diet high in fiber and low in animal protein. She would like a medication to help prevent the development of more kidney stones. She has a past medical history of asthma for which she takes a budesonide/formoterol inhaler (Symbicort) as needed. Today, her vital signs and a physical examination are normal. A recent basic metabolic panel was normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate prevention strategy? A) Lifestyle modifications only B) Furosemide (Lasix), 20 mg daily C) Hydrochlorothiazide, 50 mg daily D) Magnesium oxide, 400 mg twice daily E) Tamsulosin (Flomax), 0.4 mg daily
Explanation: Lifestyle changes such as appropriate fluid intake (2.5â€“3.0 L of liquid per day), a diet high in fiber and low in animal protein, lower sodium intake (<4â€“5 g per day), and moderate calcium intake (1200 mg per day) can help reduce recurrence of calcium stones. However, if patients continue to develop recurrent calcium stones, it is reasonable to initiate pharmacologic therapy. Appropriate medications include high-dose thiazide diuretics, allopurinol, and potassium citrate. Furosemide would not be appropriate as it has been associated with kidney stone formation. Magnesium oxide has not been shown to decrease the risk of stone development. Tamsulosin can be used to help with stone expulsion, but it does not help to prevent stones.

QuestionStem: A 72-year-old female is brought to the emergency department after her neighbor found her on the floor in her home. She is drowsy and confused, and unable to provide any history. An initial laboratory evaluation reveals the following: Glucose 95 mg/dL BUN 9 mg/dL (N 6â€“20) Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (N 0.5â€“1.1) Estimated glomerular filtration rate 63 mL/min/1.73 m2 Sodium 118 mEq/L (N 135â€“145) Potassium 3.0 mEq/L (N 3.3 â€“5.0) Chloride 88 mEq/L (N 101â€“115) Bicarbonate 22 mEq/L (N 16â€“30) Anion gap 8 mEq/L (N 5â€“14) Calcium 8.8 mg/dL (N 8.4â€“10.5) Which one of the following would be the most appropriate intervention at this time? A) Free water restriction B) Normal saline infusion C) 3% hypertonic saline bolus D) Intravenous desmopressin (DDAVP)
Explanation: This patient presents with symptomatic severe hyponatremia (sodium level <125 mEq/L). The most appropriate intervention at this time is a 100-mL bolus of 3% hypertonic saline (SOR B), which may be repeated up to 2 times at 10-minute intervals with the goal of increasing the sodium level by 4â€“6 mEq/L. Serum sodium levels should be monitored after every bolus and every 6 hours during the first 24 hours of treatment (SOR C). Avoiding rapid correction of severe hyponatremia is important to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. Depending on risk factors, sodium correction should be limited to 8â€“12 mEq/L over 24 hours or 18 mEq/L over 48 hours (SOR C). The use of normal saline is appropriate treatment for nonsevere hypovolemic hyponatremia (a sodium level â‰¥125 mEq/L but <135 mEq/L). Intravenous desmopressin (DDAVP) is indicated for treatment of diabetes insipidus, which usually presents with high- normal sodium levels or hypernatremia. Desmopressin may also be given with 5% dextrose in water if overcorrection of severe hyponatremia has occurred.

QuestionStem: A 35-year-old construction worker presents to your office with an acute onset of lower back pain that began 3 days ago. The patient describes the pain as a burning sensation that radiates down his left leg. He has no bowel or bladder dysfunction and has been in good health. A straight legâ€“raising test is positive at 45Â° on the left without motor deficit. Which one of the following does evidence support as the first step in treatment of this patient's condition? A) Bed rest for 3 days B) Mobilization and manipulation C) A short-term corticosteroid D) Cyclobenzaprine E) MRI
Reference: Klein DA, Sylvester JE, Schvey NA. Eating disorders in primary care: diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2021;103(1):22-32. | Final Recommendation Statement: Lung Cancer: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2021. | Final Recommendation Statement: Osteoporosis to Prevent Fractures: Screening. US Preventive Services Task Force, 2018. | Viera AJ, Wouk N: Potassium disorders: Hypokalemia and hyperkalemia. Am Fam Physician 2015;92(6):487-495. | Stephens MB, Wiedemer JP, Kushner GM: Dental problems in primary care. Am Fam Physician 2018;98(11):654-660. | Ross DS, Burch HB, Cooper DS, et al: 2016 American Thyroid Association guidelines for diagnosis and management of hyperthyroidism and other causes of thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid 2016;26(10):1343-1421. | Kravets I: Hyperthyroidism: Diagnosis and treatment. Am Fam Physician 2016;93(5):363-370. | American Psychiatric Association. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. 5th ed. American Psychiatric Association; 2013:123–154.
Explanation: In the absence of red flags (fracture, malignancy, epidural abscess, or cauda equina syndrome), the approach to acute lumbar radiculopathy should be conservative. Most patients improve over the first 2 weeks, and there is level B evidence that mobilization and manipulation is of benefit. Bed rest was previously recommended, but current data supports allowing the patient to be as active as the pain allows. While corticosteroids, NSAIDs, and muscle relaxants are often used, evidence to do so is lacking. In the subacute stage, neurodynamic mobilization and strength training could be added. If there is no improvement over 6 weeks, then MRI should be ordered.

QuestionStem: A 46-year-old male with chronic hypertension presents to your clinic for routine follow-up. Due to issues with insurance coverage, he has not been taking his antihypertensive medications for the last 2 months. Today, he is asymptomatic and an examination is normal, except for a blood pressure of 162/100 mm Hg, which is confirmed on repeat testing. In addition to addressing insurance and social determinants of health, which one of the following would be most appropriate for this patient? A) Monotherapy with a calcium channel blocker (CCB) B) Monotherapy with a thiazide-like diuretic C) Combination therapy with a CCB and an ACE inhibitor D) Combination therapy with a diuretic and a Î²-blocker E) Combination therapy with a diuretic and an aldosterone antagonist
Explanation: The vast majority of patients treated for essential hypertension will require 2 or more agents to achieve adequate blood pressure control. Combination therapy with a calcium channel blocker and an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) would be most appropriate for this patient with stage 2 hypertension. Stage 2 hypertension is defined as a systolic blood pressure â‰¥160 mm Hg or a diastolic blood pressure â‰¥100 mm Hg (or a systolic blood pressure >20 mm Hg above goal or a diastolic blood pressure >10 mm Hg above goal). Race-based prescribing, which advises preferential use of thiazide or calcium channel blockers for Black patients, has demonstrated insufficient blood pressure control and is thus falling out of favor. Î²-Blockers are not used as first-line treatment for hypertension, but they may be indicated in patients with specific comorbidities, such as coronary artery disease or heart failure. Aldosterone antagonists are also not typically used as first-line treatment for hypertension, but they may be used if multiple other drug classes cannot achieve adequate blood pressure control. Monotherapy is unlikely to produce adequate blood pressure control in this patient.

QuestionStem: A 14-year-old female presents to your office with her mother who is concerned about her daughter's recent 15-lb weight loss and severe fatigue. After ruling out other etiologies, you diagnose anorexia nervosa. Which one of the following findings, if present in this patient, would indicate the need for inpatient care? A) Hair loss B) Muscle atrophy C) Parotid gland enlargement D) A potassium level of 2.6 mEq/L (N 3.5â€“4.9) E) A sodium level of 146 mEq/L (N 134â€“145)
Explanation: A potassium level of 2.6 mEq/L would indicate the need for inpatient care of this patient's anorexia nervosa. Eating disorders can often be treated in the outpatient setting with a multidisciplinary care team including physicians, therapists, and dietitians. However, certain findings, including severe hypokalemia, require higher levels of care. While hair loss, muscle atrophy, parotid gland enlargement, and elevated sodium can all be found in patients with eating disorders, severe electrolyte abnormalities can lead to life-threatening cardiac complications and require immediate treatment.

QuestionStem: A 30-year-old female presents to your office for an annual wellness visit. She reports a family history of Lynch syndrome and recently tested positive herself. Which one of the following should you recommend regarding colon cancer screening in this patient?
